title: 5  Your AI Co-Pilot: Using Gemini Inside RStudio as Your Personal R Translator
section: 5.11 What This Means for an Excel Refugee
text:
5.11 What This Means for an Excel Refugee
This chapter is not really about AI fashion.
It is about workflow dignity.
In Excel, you often survive by remembering more than you should have to remember. In a browser-based chat workflow, you often survive by re-explaining more than you should have to re-explain. In a Gemini-plus-RStudio workflow, some of that wasted motion finally falls away.
The assistant is closer to the work.
The prompt is closer to the code.
The code is closer to the business rule.
That is why this chapter matters so much in the migration story.
You are no longer limited by what you can memorize under pressure. You are learning how to direct an assistant from inside the same environment where your reporting life already happens.
That is not a gimmick.
That is leverage.
And once you feel that leverage, the next question changes.
You stop asking, “Can AI help me write R code?”
You start asking, “What else in my messy, repetitive workflow can now be made lighter?”






ImportantMonday Morning Application



Choose one Excel-driven task you did this week and run it through the full Gemini-in-RStudio workflow.

state the business rule in plain English
store your Gemini key safely in .Renviron if you have not already
connect Gemini with chat_google_gemini()
pass the connection to chattr_use()
highlight the relevant code or commented instruction and send it with your shortcut
verify the result using row counts, totals, or spot checks

Then answer one question in writing.
Did keeping the assistant inside RStudio reduce friction enough to make the task feel more repeatable than before?

title: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell
text:
6.1 Dirty Data Does Not Stay in the Spreadsheet
A lot of professionals talk about bad data as if it were a technical nuisance.
It is not.
It is a trust problem.
A department name spelled three different ways does not stay a text issue. It becomes a bad headcount summary. A missing date does not stay a blank cell. It becomes a broken time trend. A duplicated employee ID does not stay a mild annoyance. It becomes a payroll reconciliation problem, a false total, a leadership question, a quiet loss of confidence in the numbers you are about to present.
This is why dirty data feels heavier than it looks.
It contaminates thought.
When the input is unstable, your mind shifts into defensive mode. You stop asking what the pattern means and start asking whether the table can be trusted. You stop moving toward interpretation and get dragged backward into repair. The analysis becomes cautious before it becomes useful.
That cost is rarely visible in a final report.
Leadership sees the finished table, the cleaned chart, the polished slide.
They do not see the hour you lost because one region was written as West, WEST, and West.
They do not see the extra check you ran because the row count felt suspicious.
They do not see the quiet decision fatigue created by hundreds of tiny corrections that never should have depended on your attention in the first place.
This is the part worth naming clearly.
Bad data does not only waste time.
It downgrades the level of work you are able to do.
You can spend a whole morning being productive and still never get to the part of the job that makes you strategically valuable.
That is what manual cleaning steals.
crumbs: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell

title: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell
section: 6.2 The Calm Sequence That Replaces Panic
text:
6.2 The Calm Sequence That Replaces Panic
The first real upgrade in R is not a clever function.
It is a sequence.
A repeatable order of operations that replaces the old improvisation.
When people stay trapped in spreadsheet cleaning too long, they start treating each ugly file as a fresh emergency. That creates chaos even before the cleaning begins. You jump from blanks to dates to duplicates to categories to formatting and back again, fixing whatever looks most offensive in the moment.
R rewards a different temperament.
You do not need more urgency.
You need a stable sequence.
A good cleaning workflow usually moves like this:

import the file
inspect the structure
standardize the column names
clean text fields
fix dates
handle missing values intentionally
remove duplicates
validate key counts or totals
save the clean output

Not because sequence is elegant for its own sake.
Because sequence lowers noise.
It helps you stop reacting and start controlling the file.
Here is the kind of setup that changes the emotional tone immediately:

library(readr)
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(lubridate)
library(tidyr)

Now imagine the dirty file arrives in two forms. Payroll comes as Excel. A separate region mapping file comes as CSV.

payroll_raw <- read_excel("data/payroll_extract.xlsx")
region_lookup <- read_csv("data/region_lookup.csv")

Then inspect before touching anything:

glimpse(payroll_raw)
glimpse(region_lookup)

That small pause matters.
Inspection is where you stop treating the file like an enemy and start treating it like evidence. What are the columns. Which fields are character. Which fields look numeric but probably are not. Which dates arrived as text. Which names will cause pain later if you do not standardize them now.
Then you begin the actual cleaning.

payroll_clean <- payroll_raw |>
  rename_with(~ str_to_lower(.x)) |>
  rename_with(~ str_replace_all(.x, "\\s+", "_"))

That pair of rename_with() calls does something spreadsheet cleaning rarely does well. It makes the structure calm. Columns stop shouting in mixed case. Spaces stop breaking code later. The file starts feeling less like a trap.
This is the first moment of relief.
Not because the data is clean yet.
Because the workflow is.






NotePhD Insight



Dirty data creates cognitive drag long before it creates a visible reporting error.
When structure is inconsistent, your brain keeps spending attention on local repairs. Which column name is correct. Whether the text field hides trailing spaces. Whether the date was parsed properly. That low-level vigilance reduces the mental capacity available for interpretation and decision support.
A repeatable cleaning sequence shifts the work from improvisation to process. That is why clean workflows feel calmer. They reduce the number of decisions future-you has to make all over again.
crumbs: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell

title: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell
section: 6.3 Stop Fixing Rows One by One
text:
6.3 Stop Fixing Rows One by One
This is where R starts feeling like power instead of promise.
In Excel, cleaning often becomes physical. You drag, copy, trim, delete, replace, filter, undo, sort, check, and repeat. The whole process depends on visual attention and manual gestures.
R moves the work up one level.
You stop cleaning row by row and start applying rules to columns and structures.
Take text cleanup. Spreadsheet exports are full of invisible spaces, inconsistent capitalization, and categories that differ just enough to break grouping later.
You can clean those fields like this:

payroll_clean <- payroll_clean |>
  mutate(
    employee_id = str_trim(employee_id),
    department = str_trim(department),
    department = str_to_title(department),
    location = str_trim(location)
  )

That single block replaces a whole family of spreadsheet habits.
No helper columns.
No fill-down formulas.
No copy-paste-values ritual.
No second workbook because you were afraid to break the first one.
Now take missing values. This is where professionals often make rushed decisions because missingness feels untidy and personal. Blank values embarrass people. They want the file to stop looking incomplete.
But not every missing value deserves the same treatment.
Some values should stay missing until someone upstream fixes the source.
Some should be replaced with a neutral label like "Unknown".
Some can safely become zero.
The point is not to “remove all blanks.” The point is to decide what a blank means in business terms.

payroll_clean <- payroll_clean |>
  mutate(
    department = replace_na(department, "Unknown"),
    bonus_amount = replace_na(bonus_amount, 0),
    manager_name = replace_na(manager_name, "Unassigned")
  )

That is not cosmetic cleaning. It is interpretation.
You are deciding what absence means before the absence quietly poisons the report.
crumbs: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell

title: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell
section: 6.4 Duplicates Are Never Just Duplicates
text:
6.4 Duplicates Are Never Just Duplicates
This is one of the places where spreadsheet culture teaches the wrong instincts.
People see duplicate rows and think, “Remove duplicates.”
Sometimes yes.
Sometimes no.
A duplicate can mean repeated export lines, amended records, a flawed join, or legitimate repeated transactions that only look suspicious because the file lacks enough context. The act of removing duplicates is not the smart part. The smart part is defining what counts as duplication for this task.
In HR or payroll work, a duplicated employee_id might signal a serious issue if the file is supposed to contain one current row per employee. In finance, repeated invoice lines might be perfectly valid if they represent different transactions.
That is why distinct() matters. It forces you to be explicit.

payroll_clean <- payroll_clean |>
  distinct(employee_id, pay_period, .keep_all = TRUE)

That says: for this report, the combination of employee_id and pay_period should be unique. Keep one row and drop the rest.
You are not merely cleaning.
You are stating the reporting logic clearly enough that the file cannot pretend ignorance.
And that is a professional move.
Not a cosmetic one.
crumbs: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell

title: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell
section: 6.5 Dates Are Where Fragile Workflows Go to Humiliate People
text:
6.5 Dates Are Where Fragile Workflows Go to Humiliate People
Few things make a corporate file feel more insulting than dates in three formats.
A payroll export arrives with 04/01/2026.
A manual adjustment tab uses 1-Apr-26.
A lookup sheet stores dates as text.
One field reads like month-day-year. Another reads like day-month-year. A third is technically numeric but only if you know what century the system thinks it is in.
This is the kind of mess that turns spreadsheet users into reluctant historians.
R will not make bad dates beautiful, but it will let you be explicit about what you are parsing.
Suppose your date field arrives in month-day-year format:

payroll_clean <- payroll_clean |>
  mutate(pay_date = mdy(pay_date))

If another field arrives as a character string but should become a monthly reporting field:

payroll_clean <- payroll_clean |>
  mutate(report_month = floor_date(mdy(pay_date), unit = "month"))

Now the file stops behaving like a collection of visual date guesses and starts behaving like a dataset that can support trends, grouping, and validation.
This matters because broken dates do not only break charts.
They break stories.
Once the timeline is wrong, every pattern you show leadership becomes harder to trust.
crumbs: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell

title: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell
section: 6.6 Categories Break Quietly
text:
6.6 Categories Break Quietly
Not every data problem announces itself with an error.
Some of the most expensive ones arrive looking almost correct.
Human Resources
HR
Human resources
Human Resources
A human reader treats these as the same idea. A grouped summary does not.
That is why category cleanup deserves more respect than it usually gets. It is not formatting. It is classification discipline.
A simple standardization step can save a report from false fragmentation:

payroll_clean <- payroll_clean |>
  mutate(
    department = str_trim(department),
    department = str_to_title(department),
    department = case_when(
      department %in% c("Hr", "Human Resources") ~ "Human Resources",
      department %in% c("Fin", "Finance") ~ "Finance",
      TRUE ~ department
    )
  )

This is where you begin to feel the difference between cleaning for appearance and cleaning for truth.
A report grouped on dirty categories will still render.
It will simply tell the wrong story politely.






TipCheat Sheet Box



The Repeatable Cleaning Sequence

import the raw file
inspect with glimpse()
standardize column names early
trim and normalize text fields
convert dates explicitly
handle missing values by business meaning
remove duplicates with a defined key
validate row counts or totals
save a clean output with write_csv()

When the sequence stays stable, future files stop feeling personal.
crumbs: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell

title: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell
section: 6.7 One Pipeline Beats Fifty Tiny Fixes
text:
6.7 One Pipeline Beats Fifty Tiny Fixes
Once you have the pieces, the real breakthrough is putting them together.
Here is what a realistic first-pass cleaning pipeline might look like for a payroll export that arrives messy on Monday morning:

payroll_clean <- read_excel("data/payroll_extract.xlsx") |>
  rename_with(~ str_to_lower(.x)) |>
  rename_with(~ str_replace_all(.x, "\\s+", "_")) |>
  mutate(
    employee_id = str_trim(employee_id),
    department = str_trim(department),
    department = str_to_title(department),
    location = str_trim(location),
    pay_date = mdy(pay_date),
    department = replace_na(department, "Unknown"),
    bonus_amount = replace_na(bonus_amount, 0)
  ) |>
  distinct(employee_id, pay_period, .keep_all = TRUE)

Then validate a couple of fundamentals before you trust it:

glimpse(payroll_clean)

payroll_clean |>
  count(department, sort = TRUE)

payroll_clean |>
  summarise(
    employees = n(),
    missing_dates = sum(is.na(pay_date)),
    total_bonus = sum(bonus_amount, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

Now you are no longer “cleaning the file.”
You are building a repeatable process that transforms the file from unstable input into trustworthy working data.
That is a different kind of confidence.
crumbs: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell

title: 6  Clean Data, Clean Mind: The R Workflow That Eliminates Manual Hell
section: 6.8 Future-You Is the Real Stakeholder
text:
6.8 Future-You Is the Real Stakeholder
This is the emotional center of the chapter.
A lot of people think the win is getting the file clean once.
It is not.
A one-time clean file is nice. A reusable cleaning script is freedom.
Because the real question is not whether you can rescue this week’s export.
You can.
The real question is whether next week’s version of the same mess should still be able to steal the same hour from you.
That is where most spreadsheet workflows fail the person doing the work. They reward immediate survival and punish future-you. Every manual fix gets buried in habit. Every workaround gets stored in memory instead of process. Every repeated rescue silently votes for the next repeated rescue.
R gives you a chance to stop voting that way.
When you save the cleaning logic as a script, you are not just documenting the work.
You are refusing to do the same avoidable labor twice.
Save the result:

write_csv(payroll_clean, "outputs/payroll_clean.csv")

Save the script.
Keep the sequence.
Name the file like it belongs to an adult workflow.
Future-you should not have to reverse-engineer what present-you did under pressure.
That is what makes scripts more than code.
They are receipts for thought.
They prove the cleaning happened in a form you can trust, rerun, improve, and hand over.

section: 6.9 The Version That Saves Hours Every Week
text:
6.9 The Version That Saves Hours Every Week
This is where the promise of the book becomes visible.
You do not save hours every week because one file happened to be cleaner.
You save hours every week because the cleaning logic stops living in your fingers and starts living in the workflow.
That shift has consequences.
Your Monday becomes lighter.
Your reports become easier to trust.
Your summaries stop breaking for stupid reasons.
You spend less time proving the file deserves attention and more time deciding what the numbers mean.
That is the professional leap hidden inside data cleaning.
The clean dataset is not the final prize.
The clean mind is.
The calm that comes from knowing the next ugly export does not get to own your morning the way the last one did.

section: 6.11 Now The Numbers Deserve To Be Summarized
text:
6.11 Now The Numbers Deserve To Be Summarized
Once the data is trustworthy, something changes in the room.
You stop defending the file.
You start using it.
That is when the real analyst work begins. Grouping. Summarizing. Comparing. Explaining. Building something cleaner than a Pivot Table that somebody is afraid to refresh.
Dirty data keeps you stuck at the level of rescue.
Clean data returns you to the level of thought.
And that is exactly where this book needs to go next.
Because once you stop fixing the spreadsheet twice, one question becomes irresistible.
If the data is finally clean, why are you still rebuilding summaries the old way?






ImportantMonday Morning Application



Choose one dirty export from your real work this week.
Then do this in order:

import it into R with read_excel() or read_csv()
inspect it with glimpse()
standardize the column names
trim text fields and normalize categories
convert one date field explicitly
handle missing values on purpose
remove duplicates using a defined key
validate one count or total
save the cleaned result with write_csv()

Do not aim for elegance.
Aim for repeatability.
If the same file arrived again next Monday, would your script know what to do before your stress did?

title: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr
text:
7.1 The Fastest Good Idea Excel Ever Gave You
Pivot tables earned their place.
That deserves to be said plainly.
For a lot of professionals, they were the first time data stopped feeling flat and started feeling interrogable. You could take a large table, drag a few fields, and suddenly see the shape of the business. Sales by region. Headcount by department. Spend by category. Volume by month. What looked like a cluttered export would snap into something readable in a minute or two.
That was not trivial.
It was a genuine upgrade in how people learned to reason with data.
Pivot tables gave spreadsheet users a low-friction way to move from raw rows to grouped meaning. They let people ask better questions without learning a formal language first. For a stage of your development, they were not a crutch. They were a doorway.
That is why this chapter is not an attack on them.
The point is not that pivot tables were bad.
The point is that they were brilliant for a phase of the work and increasingly inadequate for the shape the work has now taken.
There is a difference between respecting the tool that helped you grow and staying loyal to it after it has started costing you too much.
crumbs: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr

title: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr
section: 7.2 The Drag-and-Drop Trap
text:
7.2 The Drag-and-Drop Trap
The weakness of pivot tables is not obvious when the summary is new.
It shows up when the summary becomes recurring.
That is the break point.
A recurring report should get lighter over time. The business question stays familiar. The logic stabilizes. The workflow should become more efficient with repetition. But pivot-based reporting often does the opposite. The summary may be conceptually identical each week, yet the interface keeps dragging you back into manual reconstruction.
Refresh the pivot.
Check the source range.
Reapply the sort.
Fix the labels.
Rebuild the calculated field.
Undo the default formatting.
Copy the result into slides.
Repeat next week.
None of those steps is individually dramatic. Together, they create a strange professional insult. You are answering a known question through a process that behaves as if the question were brand new every time.
And because pivot tables live so deeply in the interface, the logic is harder to read than people admit. A workbook may show the final summary, but the reasoning behind it is scattered across field placement, hidden source settings, formatting choices, and memory. Someone else opening the file can see the output without fully seeing the thought.
That matters.
Because hard-to-read logic is hard-to-trust logic.
And hard-to-trust logic forces you back into vigilance instead of analysis.
crumbs: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr

title: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr
section: 7.3 When The Summary Knows More Than The Workbook
text:
7.3 When The Summary Knows More Than The Workbook
This is where irritation becomes useful.
A lot of recurring reports outgrow the environment that still contains them. The business question becomes stable enough to deserve a durable answer, but the workbook keeps responding with temporary behavior.
Take a weekly revenue summary by region and month. Or a monthly headcount by department and location. Or payroll totals by cost center. These are not mysterious questions. They are not experimental. They are not one-off puzzles requiring artisanal spreadsheet choreography.
They are stable summary questions.
Yet the workbook handles them as if the analyst should remain trapped inside the same repetitive movements.
That is the hidden cost of interface-first summary work. The answer may be right, but it remains awkward to audit, awkward to reuse, and awkward to adapt when the data shape shifts slightly. A new region appears. A department is renamed. A month arrives late. A field moves. The pivot still works, until it does not, and when it does not the failure is often discovered late, after you have already trusted the output enough to format it.
This is not only inefficient.
It is a weak bargain between your intelligence and your time.
You know more than the workflow allows you to express cleanly.
The summary knows what you mean. The workbook does not know how to remember it with dignity.






WarningFriction Warning



A recurring summary is no longer “quick” if it depends on all of the following every cycle:

refreshing the pivot manually
checking whether the source range moved
reapplying sorts or labels
recreating calculated fields
reformatting output before sharing
copying results into another workbook or slide deck

That is not speed. That is repetition disguised as convenience.
crumbs: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr

title: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr
section: 7.4 dplyr Lets You State The Question Directly
text:
7.4 dplyr Lets You State The Question Directly
This is where relief begins.
dplyr does not ask you to drag fields around an interface to discover what you mean. It asks you to say what you mean clearly.
That difference changes the entire emotional tone of summary work.
Suppose you want total revenue by region from a clean sales export. Start by bringing the file in and standardizing the structure.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(janitor)
library(scales)
library(tidyr)


sales <- read_csv("data/sales_clean.csv") |>
  clean_names()

Now inspect it, quickly.

glimpse(sales)

Then ask the question directly.

revenue_by_region <- sales |>
  group_by(region) |>
  summarise(
    total_revenue = sum(revenue, na.rm = TRUE),
    orders = n(),
    average_order_value = mean(revenue, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_revenue))

revenue_by_region

That is the business question, stated in readable form.
Group by region.
Summarise the revenue.
Count the orders.
Calculate the average order value.
Sort the result from highest to lowest.
Nothing is hidden in the interface. Nothing is being implied by where a field was dragged. The logic is visible, rerunnable, and easy to inspect later.
That is why grouped summaries in R feel calmer. They do not ask you to remember where the answer lives. They show you how the answer is being produced.
crumbs: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr

title: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr
section: 7.5 group_by() And summarise() Are Not Programming Tricks
text:
7.5 group_by() And summarise() Are Not Programming Tricks
This is important for the reader who still feels the old resistance rise whenever code appears.
group_by() and summarise() are not technical rituals for people who want to look clever.
They are plain business reasoning in a more durable form.
group_by() says: break the data into the groups you care about.
summarise() says: now tell me what you need to know about each group.
That is all.
If you have ever built a pivot table, you already understand the thinking. What changes is not the analytical act. What changes is the medium through which the act becomes stable.
Suppose you need monthly headcount by department.

headcount <- read_csv("data/headcount_clean.csv") |>
  clean_names()

headcount_summary <- headcount |>
  group_by(report_month, department) |>
  summarise(
    headcount = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(report_month, department)

headcount_summary

This is pivot table thinking with its spine exposed.
You are not dragging a department field into rows and a month field into columns and hoping the result survives refresh. You are saying, in full view, what the grouping is and what the summary is.
That visibility is where confidence starts returning.






NotePhD Insight



Grouped summaries are easier to verify because they make aggregation logic explicit.
In interface-driven tools, the summary can be correct without the reasoning being easy to inspect. In a grouped pipeline, the reasoning is part of the artifact. The grouping variables, the summary functions, and the ordering all live in the code. That makes the workflow easier to audit, easier to explain, and easier to rerun without memory doing half the work.
crumbs: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr

title: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr
section: 7.6 Stop Rebuilding The Same Answer
text:
7.6 Stop Rebuilding The Same Answer
This is the real breakthrough of the chapter.
Not that R can summarize data.
That R can remember how you summarized it.
Once the grouped logic is written, the weekly rebuild disappears. Not the thought. The rebuild.
The updated file arrives.
You import it.
You run the pipeline.
The summary comes back in the same form as last week, because last week’s logic is now a reusable asset rather than a sequence of clicks you have to reconstruct under deadline.
This is where a one-off report becomes a workflow.
Imagine an operations file with weekly volumes by site. In a spreadsheet ritual, you would refresh the pivot, rebuild the sort, reapply the labels, and fix the formatting if the shape changed. In R, you write it once:

operations <- read_csv("data/operations_clean.csv") |>
  clean_names()

site_summary <- operations |>
  group_by(site, week) |>
  summarise(
    total_volume = sum(volume, na.rm = TRUE),
    average_turnaround = mean(turnaround_minutes, na.rm = TRUE),
    transactions = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(week, desc(total_volume))

site_summary

Now the summary is not a fragile outcome of remembered interface moves.
It is a visible expression of the reporting logic.
That changes the relationship between you and the report. You stop feeling like its caretaker and start acting like its author.
crumbs: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr

title: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr
section: 7.7 Readable Logic Is A Professional Advantage
text:
7.7 Readable Logic Is A Professional Advantage
This is the part people often realize late.
Once summary logic becomes readable, it becomes easier to defend.
You can show a manager the pipeline and explain the grouping.
You can check the row counts and totals.
You can modify the question without rebuilding the whole object.
You can hand the workflow to another analyst without also handing over a private oral history of how the workbook behaves on bad days.
Readable logic is not only good for technical cleanliness.
It changes how your work is perceived.
You move from “the person who can always make the pivot work” to “the person who can build a reporting process others can trust.”
That is a better career story.
And it happens not because dplyr is fashionable, but because stable grouped logic is a more durable professional asset than interface fluency alone.






TipCheat Sheet Box



Pivot Table Thinking, Translated








What You Used To Do
What You Were Really Asking
dplyr Translation




Drag field into Rows
Group the data by a category
group_by()


Add values to Values area
Calculate a metric for each group
summarise()


Sort the pivot manually
Reorder groups by importance
arrange()


Add a calculated field
Create a derived metric
mutate()


Refresh the pivot
Recompute the grouped result
Re-run the script


Copy result to another sheet
Save or reuse the summary
write_csv()
crumbs: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr

title: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr
section: 7.8 One Good Summary Script Is Worth More Than Ten Familiar Clicks
text:
7.8 One Good Summary Script Is Worth More Than Ten Familiar Clicks
By the time professionals get this far into the migration, they usually stop asking whether code can do the thing.
What matters now is whether it can do the thing with less repeated strain.
That is the right question.
Because a summary script does more than reproduce a pivot table. It preserves thought. It lets the next run start with yesterday’s logic intact. It gives future-you a calmer morning.
Save the summary:

write_csv(revenue_by_region, "outputs/revenue_by_region.csv")

Now the answer is portable.
It can feed a deck. A dashboard. A monthly review. A board pack. A manager’s email. A chart that actually deserves to exist.
And the underlying logic stays where you can see it.
That is how reporting starts feeling less like performance and more like system design.
crumbs: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr

title: 7  Pivot Tables Are Dead. Long Live dplyr
section: 7.9 Your Summary Workflow Should Outlive The Current File
text:
7.9 Your Summary Workflow Should Outlive The Current File
This is where ambition starts showing up.
The summary should not depend on this week’s workbook, this week’s tab structure, or this week’s manual formatting rescue. It should outlive the file that happened to trigger it.
That is what grouped logic gives you.
A question that can survive new data.
A process that does not panic when the next extract arrives.
A workflow that gets stronger with repetition instead of more exhausting.
When people say they want to become more strategic with data, this is often what they mean without realizing it. They do not merely want prettier tools. They want their effort to compound. They want the next report to inherit the intelligence of the last one.
That is what starts happening here.
The answer stops being rebuilt by hand.
It starts being rerun by design.

Right Side: dplyr Summary Workflow - read clean data - group - summarise - arrange - save output - rerun next week

section: 7.11 Once The Summary Is Stable, The Next Problem Is The Visual
text:
7.11 Once The Summary Is Stable, The Next Problem Is The Visual
This is the handoff point.
Once the data is clean and the summary logic is stable, the next bottleneck stops being the spreadsheet. It becomes the presentation.
How do you turn grouped output into a chart that does not look improvised.
How do you move from a stable table to a visual someone higher up can read in five seconds without asking what they are looking at.
That is not a formatting problem.
It is the next stage of control.
Because once you stop rebuilding the same summary every week, one new possibility appears immediately.
You can start designing what the answer should look like.






ImportantMonday Morning Application



Choose one recurring pivot table you rebuilt recently.
Then replace it with a grouped summary script.

import the clean file with read_csv()
standardize names with clean_names()
identify the real grouping variables
write the grouped logic with group_by()
calculate the needed metrics with summarise()
sort the result with arrange()
save the output with write_csv()

Then ask yourself one question.
If this exact summary came back next Monday, would you rather rebuild it through the interface, or rerun logic you can already read?

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
text:
8.1 Why Default Charts Keep Selling You Short
The problem with a lot of spreadsheet charts is not ugliness in the dramatic sense.
It is ambiguity.
Too much clutter. Too little hierarchy. Labels competing with bars. Legends explaining what the chart should make obvious. Categories ordered alphabetically when the real story lives in the ranking. Colors chosen because they were default, not because they were useful. Titles that describe the topic without clarifying the point.
This is how a chart ends up technically present and practically evasive.
A default chart often asks the audience to do work the analyst should already have done. Find the pattern. Decode the grouping. Figure out which number matters. Guess what the title is trying not to say directly. The visual becomes a polite obstacle between the decision-maker and the insight.
That is why so many spreadsheet charts look unfinished. Not because the analyst lacks taste. Because the tool encourages speed over intention. It gives you a chart quickly, then quietly lets you stop there.
And when work is due, stopping there is tempting.
You tell yourself it is good enough. The labels are there. The bars exist. Anyone intelligent can understand it if they look long enough.
That is not the standard a leadership-ready chart should be held to.
A useful chart should reduce effort, not demand more of it.
If the audience has to be escorted through the picture, the chart is not carrying its share of the work.
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.2 The Difference Between A Chart And A Point
text:
8.2 The Difference Between A Chart And A Point
This is where visual confidence begins.
A chart is not a decorative afterthought attached to correct numbers. It is an analytical argument made visible.
That sentence changes things.
Once you stop thinking of charts as output and start thinking of them as arguments, the design questions become clearer. What is the single thing this chart needs to help the reader see. Which comparison matters most. What ordering makes the message obvious. What can be removed without losing meaning. What title tells the truth of the chart instead of merely naming the topic.
This is the line between a chart that exists and a chart that earns trust.
A leadership-ready chart usually does a few things well:
It makes the main comparison easy to see first.
It removes distractions that do not help the comparison.
It uses titles and labels that tell the reader what matters.
It chooses a chart type that fits the business question instead of whatever happened to be selected last time.
It looks calm.
Calm matters more than people think.
A cluttered chart makes the work look less certain than it is. A clean chart suggests control. Not because the audience is superficial. Because presentation quality influences how quickly credibility arrives.
This is not about visual perfection.
It is about respecting the cost of attention in a room where people are scanning for meaning, not admiring your formatting stamina.






NotePhD Insight



Good charts reduce cognitive load by aligning what the eye notices first with what the analysis wants to emphasize.
That is why ordering, labeling, color restraint, and chart type matter so much. They are not cosmetic details. They determine whether the reader spends energy decoding the display or understanding the message. A strong chart makes the intended comparison easy before the explanation even begins.
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.3 The Plot That Finally Looks Like You Know What You Are Doing
text:
8.3 The Plot That Finally Looks Like You Know What You Are Doing
This is where ggplot2 starts feeling less like a package and more like a relief.
Excel often treats charts as something generated after the fact. Pick a range. Click insert. Choose a type. Then begin fixing whatever the default version got wrong. ggplot2 works differently. It asks you to declare what should be seen.
That is the real emotional upgrade.
You are not decorating spreadsheet output.
You are mapping data into a visual structure on purpose.
The basic logic is calmer than people expect:

data
mapping
geometry
labels
theme

Start with a realistic business example. Suppose you have a clean summary file showing revenue by region.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(forcats)
library(stringr)


revenue_by_region <- read_csv("data/revenue_by_region.csv")

Let us order the regions so the chart respects the ranking instead of alphabetical habit.

revenue_by_region <- revenue_by_region |>
  mutate(region = fct_reorder(region, total_revenue))

Now build the plot.

ggplot(revenue_by_region, aes(x = region, y = total_revenue)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Regional Revenue, Highest in South and Lowest in North",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Revenue"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) +
  theme_minimal()

That chart is not yet extraordinary.
It is already better than a lot of spreadsheet defaults.
The bars are ordered by value. The title states a point, not just a topic. The y-axis is labeled in money terms. The theme is quieter. The reader does not have to fight the chart to understand what matters.
That is the first good plot.
And for many readers, it is the first time a chart feels like it belongs to the analysis rather than being stapled to it afterward.
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.4 Stop Decorating. Start Directing Attention
text:
8.4 Stop Decorating. Start Directing Attention
This is where the chapter moves from relief to control.
Once the first chart works, the temptation is to stop. Resist it.
The biggest gains often come from small choices made after the first plot appears. These are the decisions that move a chart from “pretty enough” to “decision-ready.”
Sort bars by value.
Remove unnecessary legends.
Use a title that carries the insight.
Keep the color disciplined.
Reduce visual noise.
Show the reader where to look first.
Suppose you want the same regional chart to feel more prepared for a leadership slide. Add a stronger title, drop clutter, and tighten the formatting.

ggplot(revenue_by_region, aes(x = region, y = total_revenue)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "South Leads Regional Revenue While North Continues to Lag",
    subtitle = "Latest monthly summary from the cleaned revenue pipeline",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Revenue"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

Notice what changed.
The chart did not become “fancier.” It became clearer.
That distinction matters. Professionals lose a lot of time trying to make charts look impressive when what the audience really needs is legibility. Decorative gradients, aggressive palettes, and crowded embellishments rarely make a chart feel more serious. Usually they make it feel less finished, not more.
A strong business chart is disciplined enough to stop trying to entertain.
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.5 Trends Deserve More Respect Than Default Line Charts Usually Give Them
text:
8.5 Trends Deserve More Respect Than Default Line Charts Usually Give Them
A monthly trend chart is one of the most common outputs in corporate reporting, and one of the most routinely mistreated.
Excel’s default line chart often arrives with the same problems as its bar charts. Weak titles. Busy gridlines. Overloaded legends. A line that technically moves but does not help the audience understand what changed, when it changed, or why the movement matters.
Suppose you have monthly performance data across a year.

monthly_performance <- read_csv("data/monthly_performance.csv")

Build the line chart simply first.

ggplot(monthly_performance, aes(x = month, y = revenue)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Revenue Trend",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Revenue"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) +
  theme_minimal()

Now improve it so the chart helps the room see the actual story.

ggplot(monthly_performance, aes(x = month, y = revenue, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Revenue Recovered in Q3 but Flattened Again by Year End",
    subtitle = "Monthly revenue from January to December",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Revenue"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

Again, the core change is not decoration.
It is intention.
The line chart becomes useful when it tells the viewer what movement matters and removes everything that competes with that message.
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.6 When One Chart Is Not Enough, Facets Save You From Visual Overcrowding
text:
8.6 When One Chart Is Not Enough, Facets Save You From Visual Overcrowding
A lot of messy charts are not messy because the analyst is careless. They are messy because too much has been forced into one panel.
Several departments. Too many regions. Multiple units. Too many categories competing in one chart. By the time the legend finishes explaining the colors, the audience has already lost interest.
This is where faceting becomes useful. Instead of forcing every comparison into one crowded image, you give each group its own small panel.
Suppose you are looking at attrition by unit across months.

attrition_by_unit <- read_csv("data/attrition_by_unit.csv")


ggplot(attrition_by_unit, aes(x = month, y = attrition_rate, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~ unit) +
  labs(
    title = "Attrition Trends Vary Sharply Across Units",
    subtitle = "Monthly attrition rate by business unit",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Attrition Rate"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

Facets do something spreadsheet tools often struggle to do gracefully.
They preserve comparison without forcing chaos.
The audience can scan unit by unit. Patterns stay visible. The chart breathes. That matters when the goal is not to impress with density, but to support a decision without exhausting the reader first.






TipCheat Sheet Box



Match The Chart To The Question

use a bar chart for comparing categories
use a line chart for trends over time
use facets when one chart starts carrying too many comparisons
sort categories before plotting when ranking matters
write titles that state the insight, not just the subject
remove legends when the chart already makes the grouping obvious
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.7 The Quiet Power Of Labels, Order, And Restraint
text:
8.7 The Quiet Power Of Labels, Order, And Restraint
This is where charts start looking grown-up.
Not because of advanced features. Because of restraint.
A lot of charts become weak through accumulation. Too many labels. Too many colors. Too many gridlines. Too many font sizes fighting each other. The analyst keeps adding pieces until the visual feels complete, not noticing the moment when completeness began to hurt comprehension.
In practice, a few choices carry disproportionate weight:
Order the categories by what matters, not alphabetically.
Use direct, informative titles.
Let subtitles add useful context, not filler.
Label axes in business language.
Format numbers for the audience you actually have.
Resist unnecessary legends.
Keep the background quiet.
This is why fct_reorder() and scale_y_continuous() are so useful. They let you tell the chart how to respect the question.
Here is a headcount example.

headcount_by_department <- read_csv("data/headcount_by_department.csv") |>
  mutate(department = fct_reorder(department, headcount))

ggplot(headcount_by_department, aes(x = department, y = headcount)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Operations and Sales Carry the Largest Headcount",
    subtitle = "Current headcount by department",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Employees"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

A chart like this does not look “creative.”
Good.
It looks prepared.
That is what many business audiences trust most.
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.8 Save The Plot Like It Matters
text:
8.8 Save The Plot Like It Matters
A chart often fails before the meeting because it was exported carelessly.
Too small for the slide. Too blurry in the deck. Wrong aspect ratio. Font sizes collapsing once pasted into PowerPoint. The plot looked fine in the Viewer pane and somehow became less credible the moment it left R.
That is not a minor technical detail.
Export settings are part of the professional finish.
Use ggsave() with deliberate dimensions.

ggsave(
  filename = "outputs/revenue_by_region_plot.png",
  width = 8,
  height = 5,
  dpi = 300
)

That one habit saves a surprising amount of avoidable embarrassment.
Charts are judged partly by what they say and partly by whether they arrive looking intentional. A sharp export suggests the analyst thought about the final setting of the work, not just the code that generated it.






WarningVisual Breakout



Create a visual called The Same Analysis, Two Different Levels of Credibility.
Left Side: Default Spreadsheet Chart - generic title - unsorted bars - noisy gridlines - unnecessary legend - cramped labels - weak visual hierarchy
Right Side: ggplot2 Version - insight-led title - ordered categories - clean theme - formatted axis labels - no unnecessary legend - export ready for a slide deck
The visual should show that the difference is not cosmetic vanity. It is communication quality.
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.9 The Plot Is Not There To Prove You Worked
text:
8.9 The Plot Is Not There To Prove You Worked
A weak chart often reveals a hidden mindset.
The analyst uses the visual to prove that work happened. Here is the data. Here is the chart. Here is the output. Job done.
A stronger chart does something else.
It helps the audience decide what to notice.
That is a more strategic role.
When you embrace that role, chart-making becomes less about finishing and more about directing attention responsibly. You stop asking whether the chart exists and start asking whether the message lands quickly enough to survive a meeting room, an inbox, or a slide deck full of competing priorities.
That is not design theater.
It is analytical maturity.
Because once you can clean the data, summarize it, and then present it clearly, you are no longer just producing reports. You are controlling more of how the work gets interpreted.
That is where credibility compounds.
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.10 Optional Workflow Snapshot
text:
8.10 Optional Workflow Snapshot

library(tibble)

plot_workflow <- tibble::tribble(
  ~step, ~purpose, ~risk_if_skipped,
  "read_csv()", "Import the summary data", "Chart built on stale or wrong file",
  "mutate(fct_reorder())", "Order categories intentionally", "Bars appear in unhelpful order",
  "ggplot()", "Define the visual structure", "No clear mapping from data to chart",
  "geom_col() or geom_line()", "Choose the right geometry", "Wrong chart type weakens message",
  "labs()", "State the point clearly", "Chart exists without a clear message",
  "theme_minimal()", "Reduce clutter", "Visual noise competes with insight",
  "scale_y_continuous()", "Format values for business reading", "Numbers feel raw or awkward",
  "ggsave()", "Export with professional quality", "Chart looks rushed in the final deck"
)

plot_workflow

title: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel’s Chart Wizard
section: 8.11 The Chart Should Carry More Of The Conversation Than You Do
text:
8.11 The Chart Should Carry More Of The Conversation Than You Do
That is the identity shift hiding inside this chapter.
You should not have to stand beside every chart and rescue it with explanation.
The chart should arrive ready to do its share.
Not all of it. Some decisions still require context, conversation, and judgment. But too many analysts quietly accept visuals that leave the hardest work to their voice because they have been taught that chart quality is secondary to numerical correctness.
It is not.
A chart that weakens a correct analysis still weakens the analysis in the room where it matters.
A chart that sharpens a correct analysis does something better. It helps the work travel.
And once the work travels well, you begin to feel different in relation to it. Less like the person assembling outputs under pressure. More like the person shaping what senior people are able to see quickly, trust easily, and act on.
That is not decorative skill.
That is strategic presence.






ImportantMonday Morning Application



Take one summary you already trust and build two versions of the same chart.

create the fastest acceptable chart you would normally make
rebuild it in ggplot2
reorder categories where ranking matters
rewrite the title so it states the insight
remove one unnecessary visual element
format the axis labels for business reading
export it with ggsave()

Then compare them honestly.
Which one would you rather place in front of a senior leader without explaining it first?
crumbs: 8  Charts That Actually Impress: ggplot2 vs. Excel's Chart Wizard

title: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide
text:
9.1 The Request That Used to Ruin Your Morning
At 8:11 on a Tuesday, the message arrives with the usual confidence of people who are not the ones who will have to build the answer.
Need a clean regional performance slide for the executive review by 10:00.
Actual sales are attached. Targets should be in the finance sheet. Product mapping is in the shared folder. The region names may need a quick tidy. Please send something leadership-ready.
That phrase always lands with suspicious calm.
Leadership-ready.
As if the work between raw exports and a credible slide is mostly formatting. As if the answer is already sitting inside the files, waiting for someone to combine the right tabs, stretch the right formula, and survive the right amount of spreadsheet turbulence before breakfast.
You know the older version of this morning.
Three files arrive. None of them agree on naming. One region appears as West, another as WEST, another as West. Product codes are short in one file and descriptive in another. Dates are text in one export and actual dates in the other. You start copying tabs into a workbook, building helper columns, stacking VLOOKUPs, checking row counts, repairing categories, saving backup versions, and hoping the spreadsheet does not punish you for being tired.
By 9:27, the work is already noisier than the question.
That used to be the story.
This time, the pressure is still real. The files are still messy. The deadline is still unreasonable in the way corporate deadlines often are.
But the workflow is finally calmer than the request.
You know the question before you touch the files. You know the cleaning sequence. You know where the joins happen, where the summary lives, and what kind of chart can carry the message without forcing you to narrate the whole thing by hand.
That is what makes this chapter matter.
Not because the request is glamorous.
Because it is ordinary.
This is the kind of reporting job that used to quietly ruin your morning.
Now it becomes the first project that proves something back to you.
crumbs: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide

title: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide
section: 9.2 The Question Before The Spreadsheet
text:
9.2 The Question Before The Spreadsheet
Good analysis does not begin with opening Excel.
It begins with knowing what decision the work is supposed to support.
That sounds obvious until you remember how many analysts have been trained to start with the files, not the question. The files arrive, so the work begins. Tabs open. Filters move. Copy-paste rituals start. Hours disappear. Then somewhere in the middle of the mess, the business question reappears and asks why it had to wait so long.
Do not start there.
For this project, the question is clear:
Which regions are underperforming against target, and which product lines are driving the gap?
That single sentence does real work.
It tells you what needs to be merged. It tells you what level the summary has to reach. It tells you which categories matter and which columns are background noise. It tells you what the slide should help the room see.
Without that question, you are cleaning on instinct.
With it, you are building toward a decision.
That is the first sign the reader is no longer behaving like a spreadsheet survivor. You are no longer opening files and hoping the logic reveals itself eventually. You are choosing the logic before the file gets to pull you around by the wrist.






NotePhD Insight



A decision question is not a nice extra. It is what determines the level of aggregation, the necessary joins, and the form of the final output.
When analysts skip that step, they often build technically busy workflows that answer the wrong question cleanly. A sharp question reduces wasted motion and improves the odds that the final chart will matter to the person who asked for it.
crumbs: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide

title: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide
section: 9.3 The Files Arrive And The Mess Begins
text:
9.3 The Files Arrive And The Mess Begins
For this project, three files show up.
A CSV of sales actuals exported from the reporting system.
An Excel sheet containing monthly regional targets.
A CSV lookup file mapping product codes to product lines.
Each file is useful.
None of them is ready.
The actuals file has inconsistent region names, unnecessary columns, and a date field stored as text. The target sheet uses spaces in the column names and spells one region differently from the actuals file. The product lookup has duplicate product codes where there should only be one record per code.
This is the point where Excel used to start making promises it could not keep.
You could get through it, yes. But you would do it through vigilance. Through memory. Through quiet suspicion. Through the strange professional habit of trusting nothing fully until the slide is already half built.
Now the mess still matters, but it no longer owns the whole atmosphere.
You know what it is.
It is raw material.
That is a different emotional position to work from.
crumbs: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide

title: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide
section: 9.4 When The Mess Finally Becomes A Model
text:
9.4 When The Mess Finally Becomes A Model
Start with the packages that will carry the project from beginning to end.

library(readr)
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(lubridate)
library(tidyr)
library(janitor)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(forcats)
library(here)

Now bring the files in.

sales_actuals <- read_csv(here("data", "sales_actuals.csv"))
sales_targets <- read_excel(here("data", "sales_targets.xlsx"))
product_lookup <- read_csv(here("data", "product_lookup.csv"))

Before touching anything, inspect the structure.

glimpse(sales_actuals)
glimpse(sales_targets)
glimpse(product_lookup)

That pause matters more than impatient people like to admit.
This is where you stop guessing. Which columns arrived as character. Which date fields need parsing. Which names will break a join later. Which columns are clutter. Which duplicate records deserve attention before they quietly multiply the wrong totals.
Now clean the actuals file.

sales_actuals_clean <- sales_actuals |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    region = str_trim(region),
    region = str_to_title(region),
    region = case_when(
      region %in% c("West", "West ") ~ "West",
      region %in% c("East", "East ") ~ "East",
      region %in% c("South-East", "Southeast") ~ "Southeast",
      TRUE ~ region
    ),
    product_code = str_trim(product_code),
    sale_month = floor_date(mdy(sale_date), unit = "month"),
    sales_value = replace_na(sales_value, 0)
  ) |>
  select(sale_month, region, product_code, sales_value)

Clean the target sheet too.

sales_targets_clean <- sales_targets |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    region = str_trim(region),
    region = str_to_title(region),
    region = case_when(
      region %in% c("South-East", "Southeast") ~ "Southeast",
      TRUE ~ region
    ),
    sale_month = floor_date(mdy(month), unit = "month"),
    target_value = replace_na(target_value, 0)
  ) |>
  select(sale_month, region, target_value)

Then clean the lookup file.

product_lookup_clean <- product_lookup |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    product_code = str_trim(product_code),
    product_line = str_trim(product_line),
    product_line = str_to_title(product_line)
  ) |>
  distinct(product_code, .keep_all = TRUE)

This is the stage that used to feel like invisible punishment.
Now it feels like control.
Not because cleaning suddenly became thrilling. Because the work is now visible, repeatable, and calm enough to trust.
crumbs: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide

title: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide
section: 9.5 Clean, Merge, Summarize
text:
9.5 Clean, Merge, Summarize
This is the heart of the whole project.
And it is also the place where the difference between Excel survival and R workflow becomes impossible to ignore.
In Excel, this part used to depend on improvised glue. Helper columns, copied tabs, hidden assumptions, formulas dragged down farther than seemed strictly reasonable. One mismatch in a key field, one duplicated code, one trailing space, and the chain started wobbling.
Now the relationships are explicit.
First, join product line names into the actuals table.

sales_enriched <- sales_actuals_clean |>
  left_join(product_lookup_clean, by = "product_code")

Next, aggregate actuals to the same level as the target table.

actuals_by_region_month <- sales_enriched |>
  group_by(sale_month, region) |>
  summarise(
    actual_value = sum(sales_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

Now join actuals to targets and calculate the gap.

performance_vs_target <- actuals_by_region_month |>
  left_join(sales_targets_clean, by = c("sale_month", "region")) |>
  mutate(
    target_value = replace_na(target_value, 0),
    variance = actual_value - target_value,
    variance_pct = if_else(target_value == 0, NA_real_, actual_value / target_value - 1)
  )

Validate before trusting the table.

glimpse(performance_vs_target)

performance_vs_target |>
  summarise(
    rows = n(),
    total_actual = sum(actual_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_target = sum(target_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    missing_targets = sum(is.na(variance_pct))
  )

That validation step matters because calm does not mean careless. The workflow is cleaner now, but you still owe the work a professional check. That is part of what makes the process feel safer. Trust is not assumed. It is earned in visible steps.
Now summarize the regional picture.

regional_gap <- performance_vs_target |>
  group_by(region) |>
  summarise(
    total_actual = sum(actual_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_target = sum(target_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_variance = sum(variance, na.rm = TRUE),
    average_variance_pct = mean(variance_pct, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(total_variance)

Then identify which product lines dominate in the weaker regions.

product_line_summary <- sales_enriched |>
  group_by(region, product_line) |>
  summarise(
    total_actual = sum(sales_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  group_by(region) |>
  mutate(
    share_of_region = total_actual / sum(total_actual, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  arrange(region, desc(total_actual))

This is where the project starts feeling less like a lesson and more like work you would trust.
The messy files are no longer pulling the whole process sideways.
The joins are readable.
The summary is stable.
The question is finally being answered at the speed of thought instead of the speed of spreadsheet repair.






TipCheat Sheet Box



The Full Workflow, In Order

define the business question
import every relevant source file
inspect the structure before fixing anything
standardize names, text, dates, and categories
handle missing values intentionally
join files visibly with left_join()
validate row counts and totals
summarize at the decision level
rank the important findings
turn the main message into a chart
export the summary and the visual

That is not a classroom sequence. That is working sequence.
crumbs: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide

title: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide
section: 9.6 Build The Slide They Remember
text:
9.6 Build The Slide They Remember
Analysis is not finished when the summary table exists.
It is finished when the insight can survive a room.
That means turning the central message into a visual that does not need to be rescued by your voice every ten seconds. The chart should carry the point clearly enough that the room understands where to look before anyone asks for the next slide.
For this project, the strongest visual is a variance chart by region. Leadership does not need a gallery of possibilities. They need to see quickly which regions are missing target and by how much.
Prepare the chart data.

regional_gap_plot <- regional_gap |>
  mutate(region = fct_reorder(region, total_variance))

Now build the chart.

ggplot(regional_gap_plot, aes(x = region, y = total_variance)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Southeast And North Show The Largest Revenue Gaps Against Target",
    subtitle = "Cumulative variance between actual sales and target by region",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Variance"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

That chart is doing more than existing.
It is pointing.
It tells the room where the problem lives. It respects the question that started the project. It lowers the amount of explanation you need to add verbally. That is what makes a slide memorable. Not visual drama. Compression. The feeling that the answer is finally visible enough to trust at a glance.
Now export it.

ggsave(
  filename = here("outputs", "regional_gap_chart.png"),
  width = 8,
  height = 5,
  dpi = 300
)

And save the summary table too.

write_csv(regional_gap, here("outputs", "regional_gap_summary.csv"))

This is the part that used to come apart in the old workflow. The numbers might have survived, but the handoff into presentation was always a little too dependent on patience, formatting stamina, and the hope that the workbook would behave one more time.
Now the handoff feels clean.
That is not aesthetics.
That is credibility.
crumbs: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide

title: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide
section: 9.7 What This Project Proves About You
text:
9.7 What This Project Proves About You
This is the identity section, but it only works if it stays grounded in what you actually did.
You did not learn an abstract concept and congratulate yourself for effort.
You took multiple messy files, cleaned them, standardized them, joined them, validated them, summarized them, and turned the answer into a leadership-ready output.
That is a real professional act.
It proves you are no longer dependent on spreadsheet improvisation for work of this kind. It proves you can move from raw exports to trusted insight without needing the whole morning to dissolve into helper columns and formula chains. It proves you can build a workflow that does not rely on memory as heavily as the old one did.
That changes more than the task.
It changes your relationship to the task.
You stop looking like the person who somehow survived another reporting cycle.
You start looking like the person who can design a reporting process others can trust.
That is not self-help language.
That is what capability feels like when it stops needing to apologize for itself.






WarningVisual Breakout



Create a visual called From Fragile Request To Credible Output.
Use six connected panels:

Executive Request

Raw Actuals File

Target Sheet

Product Lookup Table

Clean And Merge Workflow

Boardroom-Ready Variance Slide

The visual should show one thing clearly: the final slide is only calm because the workflow underneath it is.

title: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide
section: 9.8 This Was Not A Practice Exercise
text:
9.8 This Was Not A Practice Exercise
There is a point in a learning journey where the work stops feeling like rehearsal.
This chapter is that point.
A real question.
A real mess.
A real workflow.
A real output.
Nothing about that combination belongs to tutorial land anymore. This is not a sandbox. This is the shape of actual reporting work, handled with a level of calm that would have been difficult to imagine a few chapters ago.
That is why this chapter matters so much in the arc of the book.
It is proof that your earlier skills were not isolated tricks. Cleaning mattered because this project needed clean files. Grouped summaries mattered because the decision question demanded aggregation. Chart design mattered because the answer needed to travel into a room full of tired attention.
Now the pieces have held together in one coherent sequence.
That is a threshold.
And once you cross it, you do not look at the next messy request the same way.






NotePhD Insight



A full workflow produces more than an answer. It produces methodological trust.
When cleaning, joining, summarizing, and visualizing all live in one readable sequence, the analysis becomes easier to audit, easier to reuse, and easier to defend. That reduces dependence on memory and increases the credibility of both the output and the person who built it.
crumbs: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide

title: 9  Your First Real Project: A Full Analysis From Raw File to Boardroom Slide
section: 9.9 The Threshold Into Chapter 10
text:
9.9 The Threshold Into Chapter 10
You do not leave this project with abstract confidence.
You leave it with evidence.
Evidence that you can define the question before the spreadsheets start shouting.
Evidence that messy files can be turned into something coherent without emotional chaos.
Evidence that a repeatable workflow can carry real business work, not just small exercises designed to flatter the software.
That matters because Chapter 10 is not about one more technique.
It is about the person you are becoming once these workflows stop feeling exceptional and start feeling normal.
That is the real shift now.
You are not merely someone who has learned a few R functions.
You are becoming someone who works differently.






ImportantMonday Morning Application



Recreate one real request from your own work.

write the business question first
identify the source files involved
import them with read_csv() or read_excel()
standardize the names, dates, categories, and missing values
join the files visibly with left_join()
validate the merged result
summarize at the level the decision requires
build one chart that carries the message
export both the chart and the summary

Then answer one question in writing.
Did this feel like practice, or did it feel like work you would trust in front of someone senior?
