title: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next
text:
10.1 The Monday You Work Differently
At 8:04 on a Monday, the file arrives.
Not a dramatic file. Not a disaster file. Just the usual weekly operations export, attached to a message that sounds harmless if you have never had to build the answer yourself.
Please send the updated summary before 10.
Two months ago, that sentence would have changed the texture of the morning. You would have opened the workbook with a small flash of dread. Checked the tabs. Checked the formulas. Checked which version was most recent. Wondered whether the pivot table would survive the refresh. Saved a backup copy before touching anything important. Told yourself to stay calm while already feeling slightly late.
Now the files arrive and something inside the room stays quiet.
You open the project folder.
There is a script.
There is an inputs folder, an outputs folder, and a sequence you recognize.
You read the file in. You run the cleaning steps. You join the lookup table. You build the grouped summary. You save the chart. You export the output. You are still working. You are not improvising.
That difference is bigger than it looks.
The old workflow made every reporting cycle feel like a test of endurance. The new workflow makes the same cycle feel like a system you can operate with a clear head. The stress has not vanished from corporate life. Deadlines still exist. Stakeholders still send messy files. Last-minute changes still happen because they are apparently part of the atmosphere.
But the panic has lost its monopoly.
That is what this final chapter is about.
Not another technique. Not one more function to memorize. Not one last technical flourish before the book ends.
This is the chapter where the change becomes operational.
Because learning R is not the end of the migration.
Keeping the migration is.
crumbs: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next

title: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next
section: 10.2 The 30-Day Plan That Turns Learning Into Habit
text:
10.2 The 30-Day Plan That Turns Learning Into Habit
A lot of professional change fails for a predictable reason. The insight is real, the enthusiasm is real, the logic is real, then ordinary work returns and the old system pulls harder than people expected.
That is why this chapter does not end with vague encouragement.
It ends with a plan.
Not an aspirational fantasy where you reinvent yourself in a long weekend. A practical four-week migration plan for someone who still has meetings, deadlines, colleagues, and a real job that will keep arriving while the learning is happening.

10.2.1 Week 1: Setup And Translation
The goal of the first week is not mastery.
It is proof of entry.
Install R. Install RStudio. Create a project folder that belongs to one real workflow, not a pile of unrelated experiments. Choose one spreadsheet task you know too well. Something with lookups. Something with repeated merges. Something you have done often enough to resent.
Then translate it.
Not the entire reporting pipeline. One task.
Replace one VLOOKUP with left_join(). Replace one filtering ritual with filter(). Replace one helper-column logic chain with mutate() and a readable classification rule.
The first week matters because it breaks the emotional fiction that R belongs to some other kind of person. It starts proving that your actual work can enter this environment without turning into a science fair.
Here is the kind of simple project skeleton that changes the mood immediately:

library(here)
library(fs)

dir_create(here("inputs"))
dir_create(here("outputs"))
dir_create(here("scripts"))

That code is ordinary.
Good.
Ordinary is what you need. A place for raw files. A place for outputs. A place for logic. Structure is not glamorous. It is what keeps the next file from feeling personal.


10.2.2 Week 2: AI Workflow And Cleaning
The second week is where speed starts returning.
Use your AI assistant well. Not as a substitute for thought. As a translation layer that saves you from getting stuck in syntax anxiety. Feed it the business rule. Let it help draft the code. Verify what comes back. Keep the prompt close to the work.
This is also the week to build your first repeatable cleaning workflow.
Take one dirty export that keeps stealing time from you. Standardize the names. Trim the text. Fix the dates. Handle the missing values. Remove duplicates using a defined key. Save the cleaned output. Save the script that produced it.
The important part is not that the file ends up clean once.
The important part is that future-you no longer has to remember every fix by hand.
A first cleaning script can be as straightforward as this:

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(janitor)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)

weekly_kpi <- read_csv(here("inputs", "weekly_kpi_export.csv")) |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    region = str_trim(region),
    team = str_trim(team),
    metric_value = replace_na(metric_value, 0)
  ) |>
  distinct(report_date, region, team, .keep_all = TRUE)

write_csv(weekly_kpi, here("outputs", "weekly_kpi_clean.csv"))

That is not a monument.
It is better.
It is a workflow that can come back tomorrow without pretending it has never met you before.


10.2.3 Week 3: Analysis And Visualization
The third week is where the work starts looking more like leadership support and less like file rescue.
Take one recurring pivot table summary and rewrite it with group_by() and summarise(). Build one chart in ggplot2 that does not need to be explained apologetically in a meeting. Export both the summary table and the visual in a form that can travel into slides, email, or a shared folder.
The point here is not aesthetic self-improvement.
It is operational clarity.
Your grouped logic becomes readable. Your visual becomes cleaner. The old reporting ritual loses another piece of territory.
This is a good week to build a simple repeatable reporting pattern:

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(forcats)

weekly_summary <- read_csv(here("outputs", "weekly_kpi_clean.csv")) |>
  group_by(region) |>
  summarise(
    total_value = sum(metric_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    count_records = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_value))

weekly_summary_plot <- weekly_summary |>
  mutate(region = fct_reorder(region, total_value)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = region, y = total_value)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "West And South Continue To Lead Weekly KPI Performance",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Total Value"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

ggsave(
  filename = here("outputs", "weekly_summary_plot.png"),
  plot = weekly_summary_plot,
  width = 8,
  height = 5,
  dpi = 300
)

This is the week when the reader starts noticing a more dangerous question than “Can I do this?”
The question becomes “Why am I still doing the old workflow at all?”


10.2.4 Week 4: One Live Work Project
Week four is where everything stops being theoretical.
Choose one real deliverable. A monthly finance summary. A staffing review. A sales performance view. A branch report. A weekly KPI pack. Something with actual consequences and enough visibility to matter.
Then complete it in R.
Not necessarily every piece of the final delivery. If the workplace still expects the last mile in Excel or PowerPoint, fine. Let the old format remain the final container for now. The engine underneath is what has changed.
Import the files.
Clean them.
Join them.
Summarise them.
Chart them.
Export them.
Deliver them.
This is the week where you prove to yourself that the migration is not a side hobby.
It is a working system.






TipCheat Sheet Box



The 30-Day Migration Plan At A Glance

Week 1: set up the environment and translate one known Excel task
Week 2: build one reusable cleaning workflow and stop fixing duplicates by hand
Week 3: replace one recurring pivot-table summary and create one leadership-ready chart
Week 4: deliver one live work output from R, even if the final file is still shared as Excel or PowerPoint

The plan works because it is small enough to survive real life.
crumbs: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next

title: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next
section: 10.3 Do Not Announce The Revolution
text:
10.3 Do Not Announce The Revolution
A lot of good changes die because they are announced too early.
You do not need to deliver a speech about leaving spreadsheets behind. You do not need to tell the team you are redesigning the operating system of your work. You do not need to ask for ceremonial permission to become more effective.
Quiet change travels further.
This is the Trojan horse strategy.
Keep the output familiar if needed. Send the Excel file. Export the CSV. Drop the chart into PowerPoint. Give the manager the format they already know how to accept. Let the engine underneath evolve before the culture around you catches up.
This is not deception.
It is strategic maturity.
Organizations often resist process language long before they resist better outcomes. If the deliverable is cleaner, faster, calmer, and more trustworthy, most people do not need a philosophical briefing on how it was produced.
They need the work.
That is the smarter route for most corporate professionals. Do not fight the old system theatrically. Outperform it quietly.
The spreadsheet can remain the envelope for a while.
It does not have to remain the machinery.
crumbs: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next

title: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next
section: 10.4 The Tool Was Never The Identity
text:
10.4 The Tool Was Never The Identity
This is the shift many readers feel without being able to phrase it cleanly at first.
For years, the working identity was built around the tool.
“I do reporting in Excel.”
“I build finance packs in Excel.”
“I manage workforce data in Excel.”
“I keep the KPI tracker alive in Excel.”
That sentence structure seems harmless until you notice what it does to ambition.
It makes the tool sound like the profession.
It turns a container into a category of self.
A better identity sounds different.
“I solve reporting problems.”
“I turn raw files into usable decisions.”
“I build repeatable workflows.”
“I make analysis travel cleanly into leadership conversations.”
That identity is broader, calmer, and harder to trap.
Because once your identity shifts from software loyalty to problem-solving capability, tool choice becomes strategic instead of tribal. Excel becomes one delivery surface among others. R becomes part of the operating system. The question stops being “What kind of software person am I?” and becomes “What is the cleanest, fastest, most defensible way to solve this problem?”
That is a professional upgrade.
And it happens quietly.
No one may notice at first.
You will.
crumbs: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next

title: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next
section: 10.5 What Comes After This Book
text:
10.5 What Comes After This Book
This is where possibility opens, but it needs to open without noise.
The point is not to overwhelm the reader with a hundred technical futures all at once. The point is to show that the road does not end at cleaning a file and making a chart.
Once you have a repeatable workflow, several doors become real.
Quarto reports. So the output becomes less fragmented and more presentation-ready without a pile of manual copying.
Automated reporting. So a weekly summary can become a scheduled process instead of a recurring act of personal endurance.
Shiny dashboards. So internal users can interact with live outputs instead of waiting for static summaries.
Scheduling scripts. So the same job does not need a fresh round of effort every cycle.
tidymodels and forecasting. So the work can move from explanation toward anticipation.
Internal analytics leadership. So you stop being the person who gets handed the messy file and start becoming the person who shapes how the reporting system should work in the first place.
Not all at once.
That is the important part.
The future does not have to arrive as a technical avalanche. It only has to remain open. The book was never about turning a finance professional or HR analyst into a different species. It was about removing one ceiling and letting the next horizon become visible.






NotePhD Insight



Sustained professional change is more likely when the next horizon feels possible rather than overwhelming.
People do not persist because the future is infinite. They persist because the next step is clear enough to attempt and meaningful enough to matter. That is why a visible pathway beats vague inspiration.
crumbs: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next

title: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next
section: 10.6 Stop Returning To The Old Workflow
text:
10.6 Stop Returning To The Old Workflow
Relapse does not happen because people are weak.
It happens because pressure is real.
A deadline hits. A manager expects the familiar format. A teammate asks for the workbook. The old file is sitting there, already open, already known, already dangerous in a way that at least feels familiar. Under pressure, people return to the tool that lets them feel busy fastest, even if it is the same tool that keeps costing them hours every week.
That is why this chapter needs commitment devices.
You need practical anchors strong enough to hold when the next stressful week tries to drag you back.
A few examples matter more than people think.
Put R on LinkedIn. Not for applause. For identity reinforcement.
Save one clean project in a folder you take seriously.
Share one R-generated output with a trusted colleague.
Join one R community, even quietly.
Keep one workflow permanently migrated and refuse to move it back unless something truly breaks.
Name the new system clearly enough that future-you recognizes it as the standard, not the experiment.
A commitment device is not dramatic. It is simply something that makes reversal more psychologically expensive than continuation.
That matters because under deadline pressure, people do not rise to ideals. They fall to defaults.
Your job is to change the default.
Here is a simple way to formalize the beginning of a repeatable reporting project:

library(here)
library(fs)

project_name <- "weekly-ops-report"

dir_create(here(project_name))
dir_create(here(project_name, "inputs"))
dir_create(here(project_name, "outputs"))
dir_create(here(project_name, "scripts"))
dir_create(here(project_name, "archive"))

Now the workflow has a place to live.
That is not small.
A system becomes easier to keep when it stops looking like a temporary trick and starts looking like infrastructure.






WarningFriction Warning



The old workflow will try to return under these conditions:

deadline panic
team pressure to “just do it the usual way”
fear that the new process might take ten extra minutes today
the emotional comfort of a workbook you already know how to survive

None of these conditions proves the old system is better.
They only prove it is familiar.
crumbs: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next

title: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next
section: 10.7 A Simple Reporting Workflow That Keeps Paying You Back
text:
10.7 A Simple Reporting Workflow That Keeps Paying You Back
By now the reader has seen the pieces many times. What matters here is permanence.
A repeatable reporting workflow should be boring enough to trust.
Read the latest clean file.
Join the lookup table.
Summarise the metric.
Save the table.
Build the chart.
Save the plot.
Use the output where the business needs it.
That skeleton is not flashy. It is what frees you.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(janitor)

weekly_report <- read_csv(here("inputs", "weekly_report.csv")) |>
  clean_names()

region_lookup <- read_csv(here("inputs", "region_lookup.csv")) |>
  clean_names()

weekly_summary <- weekly_report |>
  left_join(region_lookup, by = "region_code") |>
  group_by(region_name) |>
  summarise(
    total_value = sum(metric_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    count_records = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_value))

write_csv(weekly_summary, here("outputs", "weekly_summary.csv"))

weekly_plot <- weekly_summary |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(region_name, total_value), y = total_value)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Weekly Performance By Region",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Total Value"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

ggsave(
  filename = here("outputs", "weekly_summary_plot.png"),
  plot = weekly_plot,
  width = 8,
  height = 5,
  dpi = 300
)

This kind of workflow will not win style awards from people who confuse complexity with seriousness.
It will do something better.
It will save you.
Quietly. Repeatedly. In hours no one else will ever count properly.
crumbs: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next

title: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next
section: 10.8 You Were Never Bad At Data
text:
10.8 You Were Never Bad At Data
This is where the book should end.
Not with a theatrical flourish. Not with a motivational speech pretending the workplace has become easy. It has not. Files will still arrive messy. People will still want things faster than they should. Some teams will still cling to habits that should have been retired years ago.
But the architecture has changed.
That is the point.
The problem was never that you lacked intelligence. Never that you were not “technical enough.” Never that you could not learn serious work. The problem was that you had been doing increasingly complex work inside a system too small, too fragile, and too manual for the scale of what was being asked of you.
You adapted.
Of course you did.
You became vigilant. Fast. Careful. Good at survival.
Now the work asks for something else.
Not more panic.
More structure.
More repeatability.
More visible logic.
More room for judgment to matter.
That is what this migration has been about from the start.
Not becoming a different kind of human.
Becoming harder to trap.
The Excel refugee is not someone who hated spreadsheets in the abstract. It is someone whose work outgrew the environment and finally found the nerve to admit it. Someone who stopped confusing endurance with suitability. Someone who no longer wants their best professional energy spent holding brittle systems together by hand.
That someone is not waiting for permission.
Not anymore.






ImportantMonday Morning Application



Before the next reporting cycle begins, do three things.

choose one workflow that will never go fully back to the old spreadsheet ritual
create the project folder, script, and output structure for it now
tell one other person, even casually, that you are building the next version of the work in R

Then write one sentence and keep it where you can see it.
I do not rebuild stable logic by hand anymore.
crumbs: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next

title: 10  The R-Powered Strategist: Your 30-Day Migration Plan and What Comes Next
section: 10.9 The Last Quiet Upgrade
text:
10.9 The Last Quiet Upgrade
There is one final shift worth naming.
You began this book as someone trying to escape spreadsheet chaos.
You finish it as someone capable of building professional calm.
That is not the same thing.
Escape is reactive.
Calm is constructed.
And now you know how to construct it.
That is enough.

title: The Master Excel-to-R Cheat Sheet
text:
A.1 Core Translation Map
You do not need to memorize everything in R.
You need a reliable bridge.
That is what this appendix is for. Keep it open during reporting work. Print it. Mark it up. Return to it when Excel habits are faster than confidence and the deadline is loud enough to make old workflows look tempting again.
The point here is not conceptual elegance.
The point is speed.
Use this appendix when the file is messy, the request is urgent, and your brain needs a clean translation path before 9:00 AM.
The table below is the working dictionary.
Do not treat it like theory. Treat it like operational translation. The question is not “What is the R equivalent in an abstract sense?” The question is “What do I do instead of the thing I keep doing manually in Excel?”

title: The Master Excel-to-R Cheat Sheet
text:
Excel Task
Common Excel Method
R Equivalent
Why the R Version Wins
Example Workflow




Add a total
SUM(range)
summarise(total = sum(value, na.rm = TRUE))
The logic is visible, reusable, and easy to rerun on new data without touching cells again.
Total monthly sales across all regions after importing sales_actuals.csv.


Average a field
AVERAGE(range)
summarise(avg_value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE))
Handles missing values explicitly and keeps the calculation tied to the dataset instead of a fragile range.
Average payroll by department after cleaning blanks.


Count records meeting a condition
COUNTIF(range, criteria)
filter(condition) |> summarise(n = n())
The condition is readable and easy to adjust when the rule changes.
Count employees marked "Active" in an HR extract.


Sum by one condition
SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range)
filter(condition) |> summarise(total = sum(value, na.rm = TRUE))
Separates filtering from calculation, which makes the business rule much easier to inspect.
Sum sales for the West region only.


Sum by multiple conditions
SUMIFS()
filter(cond1, cond2, cond3) |> summarise(total = sum(value, na.rm = TRUE))
Multiple rules stay readable instead of getting buried inside formula punctuation.
Sum March revenue for Region == "West" and Product_Line == "Core".


One-condition logic
IF(test, yes, no)
mutate(flag = if_else(test, yes, no))
Applies the rule across a whole column at once and makes it easier to audit later.
Flag overdue invoices as "Overdue" or "Current".


Multi-condition logic
nested IF()
mutate(group = case_when(...))
Complex classification stops looking like a formula accident and starts looking like declared business logic.
Classify employees as Core Staff, Flexible Staff, or Contractors.


Hide errors
IFERROR(formula, value)
coalesce(), replace_na(), or guarded logic with if_else()
Makes the missing or failing case explicit instead of hiding it behind a silent rescue formula.
Replace missing target values with zero before variance calculations.


Trim spaces
TRIM(cell)
mutate(field = str_trim(field))
Cleans the whole column at once, including fields imported from ugly exports.
Remove trailing spaces from region, department, or employee_id.


Join text together
CONCATENATE() or &
mutate(full_name = str_c(first_name, last_name, sep = " "))
Cleaner syntax, easier to reuse, and better for large tables.
Build a full employee name for reporting output.


Pull left, right, or middle text
LEFT(), RIGHT(), MID()
str_sub()
String extraction becomes readable and consistent across long columns.
Pull a cost-center prefix from an account code.


Find text inside text
FIND() or SEARCH()
str_detect(), str_locate()
Easier for pattern checks and more scalable when the logic must be reused later.
Detect whether a product description contains "Premium".


Format text or dates
TEXT(value, format)
format() or str_c() with cleaned fields
Keeps formatting separate from the raw value so the underlying data stays usable.
Format a report month as "Jan 2026" for a slide label.


Remove duplicates
Data tab → Remove Duplicates
distinct(key, .keep_all = TRUE)
You define what counts as duplication and keep the rule in the workflow.
Keep one row per employee_id and pay_period.


Sort data
Sort buttons
arrange(field) or arrange(desc(field))
Sorting becomes part of the logic, not an interface state that gets lost next week.
Rank regions from highest to lowest revenue.


Filter rows
Drop-down filter
filter(condition)
The filter rule becomes readable and repeatable instead of hidden in the workbook view.
Keep only "Active" employees or only "West" sales.


Conditional classification
helper columns plus IF chains
mutate() plus case_when()
Lets you create professional classification logic without dragging formulas down forever.
Group cost centers into strategic clusters.


Lookup one table into another
VLOOKUP()
left_join()
Joins are easier to audit, more stable across changing column order, and far less brittle than lookups.
Bring payroll values into a headcount file by employee_id.


Lookup with flexible column position
XLOOKUP()
left_join() plus select()
The relationship is based on keys, not where the source column happens to sit.
Match target values to actuals by region and month.


Flexible two-way match
INDEX() plus MATCH()
left_join(), filter(), or grouped summaries depending on the real task
Usually reveals that the underlying problem is relational, not cell-based.
Pull product-line names into sales actuals using product_code.


Summarize large tables
Pivot Table
group_by() plus summarise()
Summary logic becomes visible, rerunnable, and much easier to verify.
Headcount by department or revenue by region and month.


Split text into separate fields
Text to Columns
separate()
The split rule becomes reusable instead of being performed once and forgotten.
Split "Region - Department" into two columns.


Pattern-based fill
Flash Fill
mutate() with str_*() functions
You stop relying on Excel’s guess and start declaring the logic directly.
Extract initials, clean codes, or standardize labels.


Fix ugly dates
manual formatting, helper columns, trial and error
mdy(), dmy(), ymd(), floor_date()
Date parsing becomes explicit, which reduces false trends and silent errors.
Convert 04/01/2026 or 1-Apr-26 into real dates.


Handle missing values
blank cells, manual fill, ad hoc replacements
replace_na() or coalesce()
Makes the treatment of blanks visible and consistent.
Replace missing bonus values with zero and missing managers with "Unassigned".


Detect duplicates
manual scan, conditional formatting
count(key) |> filter(n > 1)
Finds repeated records quickly and helps you decide whether they are errors or legitimate repeats.
Check whether employee_id appears more than once in a supposedly unique file.


Export cleaned output
Save As, copy into new workbook
write_csv() or write.xlsx()
The cleaned result becomes reproducible and easier to hand over without workbook archaeology.
Save a cleaned payroll file for finance or export a board-ready summary workbook.




Quick Syntax Shelf
Use these as first-response patterns when the task is obvious and the deadline is not interested in your emotions.

# Import a CSV
sales <- read_csv("inputs/sales_actuals.csv")

# Import an Excel sheet
targets <- read_excel("inputs/sales_targets.xlsx")

# Clean names
sales <- sales |> clean_names()

# Trim ugly text
sales <- sales |> mutate(region = str_trim(region))

# Replace missing values
sales <- sales |> mutate(sales_value = replace_na(sales_value, 0))

# Join reference data
sales_enriched <- sales |> left_join(targets, by = c("region", "month"))

# Summarise
summary_tbl <- sales_enriched |>
  group_by(region) |>
  summarise(total_sales = sum(sales_value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Remove duplicates
unique_tbl <- sales_enriched |> distinct(employee_id, pay_period, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Export CSV
write_csv(summary_tbl, "outputs/summary_tbl.csv")

# Export Excel workbook
write.xlsx(summary_tbl, "outputs/summary_tbl.xlsx")







WarningQuick Warning



The old trap is not that Excel tasks are impossible.
The old trap is that the same task gets solved from scratch every week with no memory except your own.
If the rule can be written once, it should not be rebuilt by hand forever.

title: The Master Excel-to-R Cheat Sheet
section: A.2 Common Patterns
text:
A.2 Common Patterns
These are not polished case studies. They are repeatable field recipes. Use them when you already know the shape of the request and need a clean route from messy file to stable output.

Monthly Sales Reporting Pattern
Situation
You receive actual sales as a CSV, targets in Excel, and a product or region lookup table from another team. Leadership wants a monthly view by region with one clear summary and one chart.
What Usually Happens In Excel
You copy each file into one workbook, repair labels, build lookups, refresh a pivot, fix the sort order, and wonder whether the totals still match the original export.
Cleaner R Workflow
Import each file. Standardize names. Clean region labels. Parse the month field. Join target and lookup data. Summarize by region and month. Export the summary and the chart.
Recommended Core Functions
read_csv(), read_excel(), clean_names(), str_trim(), left_join(), group_by(), summarise(), write_csv()
Use this when the question is simple but the file chain is not. The real value here is not the formula replacement. It is that actuals, targets, and lookup logic all get pulled into one readable sequence.

library(readr)
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(lubridate)
library(janitor)

sales_actuals <- read_csv("inputs/sales_actuals.csv") |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    region = str_trim(region),
    region = str_to_title(region),
    sale_month = floor_date(mdy(sale_date), unit = "month"),
    sales_value = replace_na(sales_value, 0)
  )

sales_targets <- read_excel("inputs/sales_targets.xlsx", sheet = "Targets") |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    region = str_trim(region),
    region = str_to_title(region),
    sale_month = floor_date(mdy(month), unit = "month"),
    target_value = replace_na(target_value, 0)
  )

product_lookup <- read_csv("inputs/product_lookup.csv") |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(product_code = str_trim(product_code)) |>
  distinct(product_code, .keep_all = TRUE)

monthly_sales_summary <- sales_actuals |>
  left_join(product_lookup, by = "product_code") |>
  group_by(sale_month, region) |>
  summarise(
    actual_sales = sum(sales_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(
    sales_targets |>
      group_by(sale_month, region) |>
      summarise(target_sales = sum(target_value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop"),
    by = c("sale_month", "region")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    target_sales = replace_na(target_sales, 0),
    variance = actual_sales - target_sales
  ) |>
  arrange(sale_month, region)

write_csv(monthly_sales_summary, "outputs/monthly_sales_summary.csv")



HR Headcount Reconciliation Pattern
Situation
You receive one headcount extract from HR and another staffing file from payroll or workforce planning. The task is to reconcile counts by department and identify mismatches or missing categories.
What Usually Happens In Excel
You sort by employee ID, highlight duplicates, repair names manually, use a lookup to pull fields across, then spend too long checking whether the mismatches are real or workbook-induced.
Cleaner R Workflow
Import both tables. Clean names. Trim IDs and department labels. Remove duplicate rows by a defined key. Join staffing attributes into the headcount table. Group by department. Compare counts.
Recommended Core Functions
read_csv(), clean_names(), str_trim(), distinct(), left_join(), group_by(), summarise()
This workflow is less about clever code than about calming suspicion. You need one table that tells you what HR says, what payroll says, and where the counts drift apart.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(janitor)

headcount_hr <- read_csv("inputs/hr_headcount.csv") |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    employee_id = str_trim(employee_id),
    department = str_to_title(str_trim(department))
  ) |>
  distinct(employee_id, .keep_all = TRUE)

staffing_reference <- read_csv("inputs/payroll_staffing_reference.csv") |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    employee_id = str_trim(employee_id),
    payroll_department = str_to_title(str_trim(payroll_department))
  ) |>
  distinct(employee_id, .keep_all = TRUE)

headcount_reconciliation <- headcount_hr |>
  left_join(staffing_reference, by = "employee_id") |>
  mutate(
    department_match = if_else(department == payroll_department, "Match", "Mismatch")
  )

department_reconciliation_summary <- headcount_reconciliation |>
  group_by(department) |>
  summarise(
    hr_count = n(),
    payroll_count = sum(!is.na(payroll_department)),
    mismatches = sum(department_match == "Mismatch", na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mismatches), department)

write_csv(department_reconciliation_summary, "outputs/headcount_reconciliation_summary.csv")



Budget vs Actual Workflow
Situation
Finance needs a monthly budget-versus-actual view by cost center, with variance and variance percentage.
What Usually Happens In Excel
You open budget and actual files side by side, line up cost centers, repair formatting differences, use SUMIFS, then build a variance column and hope the formulas all extended correctly.
Cleaner R Workflow
Import both files. Clean names. Standardize cost-center labels and month fields. Join budget to actuals. Calculate variance and variance percentage. Rank the largest overruns. Export for leadership review.
Recommended Core Functions
read_excel(), read_csv(), clean_names(), mutate(), left_join(), group_by(), summarise(), arrange()
This is one of the most common “looks simple, eats a morning” workflows in corporate finance. The gain comes from making cost center logic, month alignment, and variance math visible all at once.

library(readxl)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(lubridate)
library(janitor)

budget <- read_excel("inputs/budget.xlsx", sheet = "Budget") |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    cost_center = str_to_upper(str_trim(cost_center)),
    report_month = floor_date(mdy(month), unit = "month"),
    budget_value = replace_na(budget_value, 0)
  )

actuals <- read_csv("inputs/actuals.csv") |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    cost_center = str_to_upper(str_trim(cost_center)),
    report_month = floor_date(mdy(transaction_date), unit = "month"),
    actual_value = replace_na(actual_value, 0)
  )

budget_vs_actual <- actuals |>
  group_by(report_month, cost_center) |>
  summarise(actual_value = sum(actual_value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(
    budget |>
      group_by(report_month, cost_center) |>
      summarise(budget_value = sum(budget_value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop"),
    by = c("report_month", "cost_center")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    budget_value = replace_na(budget_value, 0),
    variance = actual_value - budget_value,
    variance_pct = if_else(budget_value == 0, NA_real_, variance / budget_value)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(variance))

write_csv(budget_vs_actual, "outputs/budget_vs_actual_summary.csv")



Weekly KPI Reporting Pattern
Situation
A recurring weekly KPI file arrives from operations or reporting. The same columns need cleaning every time, and the same summary gets rebuilt every week.
What Usually Happens In Excel
You delete empty rows, trim labels, fix dates, rebuild a pivot table, copy the result into another workbook or PowerPoint slide, and repeat next week.
Cleaner R Workflow
Treat the KPI file as a standing pipeline. Import, clean names, trim text, handle missing values, summarize by site or region, export the table, save the plot.
Recommended Core Functions
read_csv(), clean_names(), str_trim(), replace_na(), group_by(), summarise(), write_csv()
This is the kind of workflow that becomes more valuable the less dramatic it feels. If the same KPI file keeps arriving, the right outcome is not excitement. It is calm repetition.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(janitor)

weekly_kpi <- read_csv("inputs/weekly_kpi_export.csv") |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    region = str_to_title(str_trim(region)),
    site = str_trim(site),
    metric_name = str_trim(metric_name),
    metric_value = replace_na(metric_value, 0)
  ) |>
  distinct(report_date, region, site, metric_name, .keep_all = TRUE)

weekly_kpi_summary <- weekly_kpi |>
  group_by(report_date, region) |>
  summarise(
    total_metric_value = sum(metric_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    records = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(report_date, region)

write_csv(weekly_kpi_summary, "outputs/weekly_kpi_summary.csv")



Department Summary Pipeline
Situation
You need one clean departmental summary for headcount, spend, attrition, or workload, often by month or quarter.
What Usually Happens In Excel
You filter a large table, build a pivot, manually rename the values area, adjust sorting, and copy the output into a slide where the formatting never quite holds.
Cleaner R Workflow
Import the cleaned source file, standardize department values, group by department and time period, calculate the needed totals or counts, sort, and export.
Recommended Core Functions
read_csv(), mutate(), str_trim(), group_by(), summarise(), arrange()
A department summary is often one of those tasks that looks too small to deserve a script until you realize you have built it eighteen times already.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(lubridate)
library(janitor)

department_metrics <- read_csv("inputs/department_metrics.csv") |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    department = str_to_title(str_trim(department)),
    report_month = floor_date(mdy(report_date), unit = "month"),
    metric_value = replace_na(metric_value, 0)
  )

department_summary <- department_metrics |>
  group_by(report_month, department) |>
  summarise(
    total_metric_value = sum(metric_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    records = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(report_month, desc(total_metric_value))

write_csv(department_summary, "outputs/department_summary.csv")



Payroll Cleanup Workflow
Situation
Payroll sends an export with duplicate employee IDs, ugly department values, missing manager names, and dates that arrive half as text and half as actual dates.
What Usually Happens In Excel
You delete rows, use helper columns, patch categories, repair dates manually, then save a “clean” version that no one can fully explain next week.
Cleaner R Workflow
Import the export. Clean names. Trim and standardize key fields. Replace missing values intentionally. Convert dates explicitly. Remove duplicates using employee and pay period as a rule. Save the clean file.
Recommended Core Functions
read_excel(), clean_names(), str_trim(), replace_na(), mdy() or dmy(), distinct(), write_csv(), write.xlsx()
This is where many spreadsheet users first feel the emotional payoff of a script. The file is still ugly. You are simply no longer negotiating with it by hand.

library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(lubridate)
library(tidyr)
library(janitor)
library(readr)
library(openxlsx)

payroll_raw <- read_excel("inputs/payroll_extract.xlsx") |>
  clean_names()

payroll_clean <- payroll_raw |>
  mutate(
    employee_id = str_trim(employee_id),
    department = str_to_title(str_trim(department)),
    location = str_trim(location),
    manager_name = replace_na(manager_name, "Unassigned"),
    pay_date = mdy(pay_date),
    pay_period = floor_date(pay_date, unit = "month"),
    bonus_amount = replace_na(bonus_amount, 0)
  ) |>
  distinct(employee_id, pay_period, .keep_all = TRUE)

payroll_validation <- payroll_clean |>
  summarise(
    clean_rows = n(),
    total_base_pay = sum(base_pay, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_bonus = sum(bonus_amount, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

write_csv(payroll_clean, "outputs/payroll_clean.csv")
write.xlsx(
  list(
    payroll_clean = payroll_clean,
    validation = payroll_validation
  ),
  file = "outputs/payroll_clean.xlsx"
)



Executive Slide Preparation Pattern
Situation
The summary is complete, but it still needs to become something a senior audience can read quickly.
What Usually Happens In Excel
You build a chart, fight the default formatting, resize it for PowerPoint, and then explain verbally what the chart should have shown more clearly.
Cleaner R Workflow
Start from the trusted summary table. Order the categories. Build one clear chart. Use a title that states the message. Export at slide-ready resolution.
Recommended Core Functions
ggplot(), geom_col(), labs(), theme_minimal(), ggsave()
This pattern is not about design vanity. It is about making the output carry more of the conversation so you do not have to rescue the slide with explanation.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(forcats)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(openxlsx)

regional_gap <- read_csv("inputs/regional_gap_summary.csv") |>
  mutate(region = fct_reorder(region, total_variance))

gap_chart <- ggplot(regional_gap, aes(x = region, y = total_variance)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Southeast And North Require Immediate Revenue Attention",
    subtitle = "Cumulative variance against target by region",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Variance"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "outputs/regional_gap_chart.png",
  plot = gap_chart,
  width = 8,
  height = 5,
  dpi = 300
)

write.xlsx(regional_gap, "outputs/regional_gap_slide_support.xlsx")

title: The Master Excel-to-R Cheat Sheet
section: A.3 Fast Decision Table
text:
A.3 Fast Decision Table
Use this when the old workflow starts whispering that the spreadsheet route will be “quicker this one time.”








If you usually do this in Excel…
Stop doing this
Start doing this in R




copy formulas down a whole column manually
trusting the drag handle to carry the rule safely
use mutate() so the rule is applied once, visibly, across the full column


rebuild the same pivot table every week
treating a recurring summary like a fresh event
write the grouped summary once with group_by() and summarise()


fix dates by hand until they “look right”
relying on cell formatting to hide date confusion
parse the dates explicitly with mdy(), dmy(), or ymd()


remove duplicates by clicking through the Data tab
dropping rows without a reusable definition of duplication
use distinct() with the actual business key


keep saving final_v2_USE_THIS.xlsx
confusing versioning with workflow memory
save the script and export a fresh output from the same logic


clean the same CSV every Monday
spending judgment on repeated repair
build one cleaning pipeline and rerun it on the new file


use VLOOKUP() because it is familiar
tying the logic to column position and workbook layout
use left_join() so the relationship stays key-based and readable


rely on hidden helper columns
letting important logic disappear into workbook archaeology
keep the transformations in a script where you can read them


sort the output manually after every refresh
letting order live in the interface instead of the workflow
use arrange() so the order survives every rerun


copy a chart into PowerPoint and fix it there
repairing a weak visual downstream
build the chart clearly in R and export it once with ggsave()


use Flash Fill because the pattern is obvious to you
trusting Excel’s guess without preserving the rule
write the rule with str_*() functions or mutate()


keep Excel as the only place the process exists
making your memory the real system
let Excel remain a delivery format while R becomes the engine

title: The Master Excel-to-R Cheat Sheet
section: A.4 Monday Morning Rescue Card
text:
A.4 Monday Morning Rescue Card






ImportantBefore You Open Excel Again



Ask these six questions first.

Is this task repeated weekly or monthly?
Will someone ask for this same summary again?
Are multiple files involved?
Are formulas breaking, drifting, or being copied down manually?
Is manual cleaning taking more than 20 minutes?
Should this be an R workflow instead?

If the answer to three or more is yes, you are probably not looking at a spreadsheet task anymore.
You are looking at a workflow that wants a script.



Fast Response Scaffold
Use this when the request is urgent and your brain needs a clean starting point.

library(readr)
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(janitor)
library(openxlsx)

ops_kpi_raw <- read_csv("inputs/weekly_ops_kpi.csv") |>
  clean_names()

region_lookup <- read_excel("inputs/finance_region_lookup.xlsx", sheet = "Regions") |>
  clean_names()

glimpse(ops_kpi_raw)
glimpse(region_lookup)

ops_kpi_clean <- ops_kpi_raw |>
  mutate(
    report_date = as.Date(report_date),
    region_code = str_trim(region_code),
    branch_name = str_to_title(str_trim(branch_name)),
    kpi_name = str_trim(kpi_name),
    kpi_value = replace_na(kpi_value, 0)
  )

regional_kpi_summary <- ops_kpi_clean |>
  left_join(region_lookup, by = "region_code") |>
  mutate(
    region = replace_na(region, "Unmapped Region")
  ) |>
  group_by(report_date, region) |>
  summarise(
    total_kpi_value = sum(kpi_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    branches_reported = n_distinct(branch_name),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(report_date, region)

write_csv(regional_kpi_summary, "outputs/regional_kpi_summary.csv")
write.xlsx(regional_kpi_summary, "outputs/regional_kpi_summary.xlsx")

# Save this script before closing the session so the next urgent request
# starts from a workflow, not from memory.



Export Decision Shelf
Use the output format that matches the audience, not the habit.

# CSV for clean downstream use
write_csv(final_summary, "outputs/final_summary.csv")

# Excel when the audience still expects a workbook
write.xlsx(final_summary, "outputs/final_summary.xlsx")



Last Quiet Reminder
The old workflow feels fast because you already know how to survive it.
That is not the same as it being the best route.
This appendix exists for the moment when habit starts impersonating necessity.
Use the map. Save the hour. Keep the logic.

title: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R
text:
B.1 Formula Translation Prompt
This appendix is built for one kind of moment.
You are already inside RStudio. The file is open. The deadline is close enough to change your posture. You do not want another browser tab, another copy-paste ritual, or another round of rebuilding context by hand just to ask for help.
You want the prompt to stay close to the work.
That is the real point of this appendix.
Not generic AI enthusiasm. Not a list of clever one-liners. A working prompt library for Gemini inside RStudio through ellmer and chattr, built for professionals who want lower friction at the point of work.
Use it when the code is selected, the comments are written, and the question needs to be asked without leaving the IDE.
This section exists for the moment when a spreadsheet rule is clear in your head but not yet clear in R.
The mistake most people make here is asking for syntax too early. They paste the formula, say “convert this,” and hope the model guesses the business meaning correctly.
That is not a strong prompt.
A strong prompt begins with the rule.
Name the table. Name the column. State what the logic is trying to do. Ask for tidyverse code, a plain-language explanation, and a quick way to verify the result.
crumbs: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R

title: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R
text:
Nested IF To case_when()
Use This When
You have a long nested IF() formula that classifies records into categories such as employment type, invoice status, performance band, branch risk level, or customer segment.
Prompt
I am moving a recurring Excel workflow into R inside RStudio.

My table is called `employees`.

I need to create a new column called `status_group` based on the column `employment_status`.

Business rule:
- If `employment_status` is "Full Time", label it "Core Staff"
- If it is "Part Time", label it "Flexible Staff"
- If it is "Contract", label it "Contractors"
- Otherwise label it "Other"

Write tidyverse code using `mutate()` and `case_when()`.

Explain each line in plain language.

Then show me one quick way to verify the output by counting how many records fall into each new group.
Why This Prompt Works
It starts with the table, the column, and the business rule before asking for code. It reduces ambiguity and tells the model what the result should mean, not just what syntax to imitate.
What A Good Result Should Include

a mutate() step
a readable case_when() block
a short explanation in plain language
a verification pattern such as count(status_group)



Basic Formula Logic To mutate()
Use This When
You have an Excel formula that calculates a derived value, such as margin, overtime cost, days overdue, or budget variance.
Prompt
I am translating an Excel calculation into R.

My table is called `finance_data`.

I need a new column called `variance` calculated as `actual_spend - budget_spend`.

Write tidyverse code using `mutate()`.

Then show me how to create a second column called `variance_pct` as the percentage difference from budget.

Please explain the code in plain business language and include one quick validation check for totals.
Why This Prompt Works
It asks for the arithmetic rule directly and requests the second metric explicitly. The model does not have to infer the business meaning from a formula fragment alone.
What A Good Result Should Include

one mutate() step or two clean steps
explicit handling of division in variance_pct
plain-language explanation
a quick validation check such as summarise(total_variance = sum(variance, na.rm = TRUE))



Text Classification Rule Prompt
Use This When
You need to classify text values into reporting groups, such as mapping free-text department labels to official categories.
Prompt
I have a table called `hr_records` and a text column called `department_raw`.

I want a new cleaned column called `department_clean`.

Business rule:
- Treat "HR", "Human Resources", and "Human resources " as "Human Resources"
- Treat "Fin" and "Finance" as "Finance"
- Keep other values as they are after trimming spaces

Write tidyverse code using `mutate()`, `str_trim()`, and `case_when()`.

Explain the code and then show me how to count the final department values for review.
Why This Prompt Works
It gives the assistant the messy inputs and the canonical outputs. That is what a real classification problem needs.
What A Good Result Should Include

str_trim() before classification
case_when() with explicit groups
a review step such as count(department_clean, sort = TRUE)



Status Grouping Prompt
Use This When
You need to convert operational statuses into reporting groups that leadership can actually read.
Prompt
I need to simplify raw workflow statuses into a reporting group in R.

The table is `operations_cases`.

The source column is `raw_status`.

Create a new column called `reporting_status` using these rules:
- "Completed" and "Closed" become "Resolved"
- "Open" and "Pending" become "In Progress"
- "Cancelled" becomes "Dropped"
- Anything else becomes "Other"

Use tidyverse code and explain the logic.

Show me one quick summary table to verify the new categories.
Why This Prompt Works
It is business-facing and explicit. It asks for the reporting translation, not for “some code.”
What A Good Result Should Include

a case_when() block
a grouped verification summary such as count(reporting_status)



Date Correction Prompt
Use This When
Dates are arriving in ugly or inconsistent text format and you need a reliable reporting month or date field.
Prompt
I have a table called `sales_actuals` with a date column called `sale_date`.

The values look like month-day-year text, for example `04/01/2026`.

Write tidyverse code that converts `sale_date` into a real date using `lubridate`, then creates a new column called `sale_month` for the first day of that month.

Explain each step and show me how to verify how many dates failed to parse.
Why This Prompt Works
It tells the model the input shape, the parsing expectation, and the final reporting field needed.
What A Good Result Should Include

mdy() or another appropriate parser
a monthly field such as floor_date()
a validation step checking is.na() counts



Helper-Column Logic Made Readable
Use This When
A workbook relies on several helper columns to build one result, and you want the same logic expressed more clearly in R.
Prompt
I have an Excel workflow with helper-column logic that does three things:
1. trims an ID field
2. classifies a status
3. calculates a variance value

I want the same logic rewritten in one readable tidyverse pipeline.

Use a table called `project_data` with columns `employee_id`, `status`, `actual_value`, and `target_value`.

Write the code using `mutate()`, `str_trim()`, and `case_when()`.

Explain the steps in plain language and tell me one quick way to validate the final output.
Why This Prompt Works
It tells the assistant the helper columns are not random. They are sequence logic that should be brought together into one readable pipeline.
What A Good Result Should Include

one clear mutate() block or a short readable pipeline
trimmed text, classification logic, and calculation logic
a verification idea such as checking counts or totals
crumbs: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R

title: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R
section: B.2 Join Logic Prompt
text:
B.2 Join Logic Prompt
This section is for the moment when you know a lookup is happening, but you want the relationship expressed in a way that is stable, visible, and easy to check.
Do not start with the words “convert my VLOOKUP.”
Start with the tables, the shared key, the fields you want to bring across, and what you need to verify after the join.

One-To-One Join Prompt
Use This When
Each record in one table should match exactly one record in another table, such as employee ID to payroll detail or product code to product line.
Prompt
I am joining two tables in R.

Table 1 is `headcount` and contains `employee_id`, `department`, and `location`.

Table 2 is `payroll` and contains `employee_id`, `salary`, and `pay_grade`.

I want to bring salary and pay grade into the headcount table using `employee_id`.

Write tidyverse code using `left_join()`.

Then show me how to verify:
1. the row count did not change unexpectedly
2. which employee IDs did not find a payroll match
What To Verify After The Join

original row count versus joined row count
any unexpected missing values in joined fields
whether the key truly behaves like a one-to-one relationship

Common Failure Signal
The joined table has more rows than the original, or too many NA values appear in the imported columns.


One-To-Many Join Prompt
Use This When
One table contains a unique master record, and the other contains repeated transactions or repeated lines for each key.
Prompt
I have a one-to-many join problem in R.

Table 1 is `employees` with one row per employee.

Table 2 is `training_records` with multiple rows per employee because each employee can attend more than one training.

I want to join the training records into the employee table, but I need help understanding how the row count will change and how to avoid misreading the result.

Write tidyverse code and explain what a one-to-many join means in business terms.

Then show me how to count how many training records each employee has after the join.
What To Verify After The Join

whether row expansion is expected
whether repeated rows reflect real repeated transactions or accidental duplication
whether a post-join grouped count makes sense

Common Failure Signal
The analyst treats the expanded row count like an error when it is actually a property of the relationship, or misses real accidental duplicates because no post-join count was run.


Mismatched Key Names Prompt
Use This When
The same identifier exists in both tables but under different column names.
Prompt
I need to join two tables in R, but the key names do not match.

Table 1 is `sales_actuals` with the column `product_code`.

Table 2 is `product_lookup` with the column `prod_code`.

I want to bring `product_line` into the sales table.

Write tidyverse code using `left_join()` and the correct key mapping.

Then show me one quick way to check whether any product codes failed to match.
What To Verify After The Join

unmatched rows in the new joined fields
whether the mapped key names are correct
whether trimming or standardization is needed before the join

Common Failure Signal
The join runs without error, but the imported column is mostly missing because the key mapping was correct in syntax and wrong in data hygiene.


Missing Join Matches Prompt
Use This When
Some records will not find a match, and you need help identifying them instead of pretending the join worked cleanly.
Prompt
I have already joined two tables in R, but some matches are missing.

The joined table is called `sales_enriched`.

The column I expected to populate is `product_line`, but some rows are `NA`.

Show me tidyverse code to isolate the unmatched rows and help me diagnose whether the issue is missing lookup values, trailing spaces, or a key mismatch.
What To Verify After The Join

the number of unmatched rows
the specific keys that failed
whether the failure is data-quality related or structure related

Common Failure Signal
You accept the NA values without checking the business impact, and the missing categories later distort the summary.


Post-Join Checking Prompt
Use This When
The join ran, but the result feels suspicious and you want a structured verification prompt, not blind trust.
Prompt
I joined two tables in R and need a professional validation checklist in code.

The original table is `actuals_by_region_month`.

The joined table is `performance_vs_target`.

Please write tidyverse checks for:
1. row count before and after the join
2. missing target values after the join
3. a quick grouped summary to see whether totals still look reasonable

Explain why each check matters.
What To Verify After The Join

row count stability
missing joined fields
total-level reasonableness

Common Failure Signal
The join “works,” but the analyst has no evidence that it worked responsibly.






NotePhD Insight



Most join errors are not syntax errors.
They are relationship errors, key hygiene errors, or trust errors. The code can run perfectly while the business logic fails quietly. That is why good join prompts ask not only for the join itself, but also for the verification pattern that follows it.
crumbs: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R

title: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R
section: B.3 Summary Prompt
text:
B.3 Summary Prompt
This section is for the old pivot-table habit.
The point is not to ask the assistant for “a summary.” The point is to state the business grouping clearly enough that the assistant can draft a summary pipeline you can reuse next week without rebuilding it.

Totals By Region Prompt
Use This When
You need a leadership summary of total sales, spend, headcount, or volume by region.
Prompt
I want the R equivalent of a recurring regional summary.

My table is called `sales_actuals`.

I need total revenue by `region`.

Write tidyverse code using `group_by()` and `summarise()`.

Also sort the result from highest to lowest revenue and explain each line in plain language.
Desired Output Shape
One row per region, one total value column, sorted from highest to lowest.
Quick Validation Check
Compare the summed total in the summary table to the total revenue in the original table.


Averages By Department Prompt
Use This When
You need the average of a metric such as salary, cycle time, resolution time, or unit cost by department.
Prompt
I need a grouped average in R.

My table is called `hr_metrics`.

I want average salary by `department`.

Write tidyverse code using `group_by()` and `summarise()`.

Please include `na.rm = TRUE`, sort the result from highest to lowest, and show me one quick way to check how many records were used in each department.
Desired Output Shape
One row per department, with an average column and often a count column.
Quick Validation Check
Include a grouped count so the average is not read without knowing how many records it came from.


Counts By Status Prompt
Use This When
You need headcount or case counts by status group, issue type, employee category, or workflow stage.
Prompt
I need a status summary in R.

My table is called `operations_cases`.

I want a count of records by `reporting_status`.

Write tidyverse code using `group_by()` and `summarise()` with `n()`.

Sort the result from highest to lowest count and explain the result in plain language.
Desired Output Shape
One row per status group with a count column.
Quick Validation Check
The grouped counts should add up to the total number of rows in the source table.


Grouped Monthly Summary Prompt
Use This When
You need a recurring summary by both month and another business grouping, such as region or department.
Prompt
I need a monthly grouped summary in R.

My table is called `performance_vs_target`.

I want total actual value by `sale_month` and `region`.

Write tidyverse code using `group_by()` and `summarise()`.

Then arrange the result by month and region, and explain what the final output will look like.
Desired Output Shape
One row per month-region combination, with a clear summary metric.
Quick Validation Check
Check that the monthly totals sum back to the grand total in the source file.


Multiple Summary Metrics Prompt
Use This When
You need more than one value in the summary output, such as total revenue, average transaction size, and row count.
Prompt
I need a reusable grouped summary in R with multiple metrics.

My table is called `sales_actuals`.

Group by `region`.

Create these output columns:
- `total_revenue` as the sum of `revenue`
- `average_order_value` as the mean of `revenue`
- `orders` as the record count

Use tidyverse code, sort by `total_revenue`, and explain how to verify the result.
Desired Output Shape
One row per group with several clearly named metric columns.
Quick Validation Check
Check whether orders matches the number of underlying records per region and whether total_revenue sums to the grand total.






TipCheat Sheet Box



Summary prompts work best when you specify four things.

the table name
the grouping columns
the metric columns
the final shape you want back

That is the difference between “help me summarize this” and a prompt that produces something you can rerun.
crumbs: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R

title: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R
section: B.4 Validation Prompt
text:
B.4 Validation Prompt
This section keeps the workflow professional.
The assistant is useful. It is not accountable. The prompt library should lower friction, not lower standards. Use these prompts when the code ran but you still need to know whether the result deserves trust.

Row-Count Check After A Join
Use This When
A join completed successfully, but you need to confirm whether the number of rows still makes sense.
Prompt
I joined two tables in R and want to verify whether the row count changed appropriately.

The original table is `headcount`.

The joined table is `headcount_payroll`.

Write tidyverse code to compare row counts before and after the join and explain what kinds of row-count changes are normal versus suspicious.
What You Are Checking
Whether the join preserved the original row count when expected, or expanded it for a reason you understand.
Why This Prevents Careless Work
Because a join that changes row count silently can distort every downstream summary.


Missing-Value Check Prompt
Use This When
A joined or transformed field may have introduced NA values that were not present before.
Prompt
I need to check whether my R workflow introduced missing values in an important field.

The table is `performance_vs_target`.

I want to inspect missing values in `target_value` and `variance_pct`.

Write tidyverse code to count missing values and isolate the rows where they occur.

Then explain what the likely causes could be.
What You Are Checking
Whether missing values reflect real missing data, failed joins, or bad transformations.
Why This Prevents Careless Work
Because missing values often look harmless until they erase categories or distort a summary.


Total Comparison Prompt
Use This When
You need to confirm that the total in a grouped summary still matches the total in the source table.
Prompt
I created a grouped summary in R and need to verify that the total still matches the source data.

The source table is `sales_actuals`.

The grouped table is `revenue_by_region`.

Write tidyverse code to compare the source total revenue to the grouped total revenue and explain what a mismatch would mean.
What You Are Checking
Whether aggregation preserved the total correctly.
Why This Prevents Careless Work
Because grouped tables can look clean and still be wrong if rows were lost, duplicated, or filtered unintentionally.


Spot-Check Prompt
Use This When
The output looks plausible, but you want a few record-level checks before trusting the whole thing.
Prompt
I want to spot-check my R output against the original data.

The source table is `sales_enriched`.

The summary table is `product_line_summary`.

Show me a tidyverse way to inspect a few rows for one region and compare them to the grouped result.

Explain why spot-checking is still useful even when the code looks correct.
What You Are Checking
Whether the grouped result aligns with recognizable underlying rows.
Why This Prevents Careless Work
Because one or two well-chosen checks often catch problems that totals alone miss.


Suspicious Result Prompt
Use This When
The output looks wrong, but you cannot immediately explain why.
Prompt
My R summary ran without an error, but the result looks suspicious.

The table is `regional_gap`.

The region totals seem too large.

Please help me diagnose possible causes using tidyverse checks for:
- duplicate rows
- join expansion
- missing filters
- incorrect grouping level

Explain each diagnostic step in plain language.
What You Are Checking
Whether the issue is structural rather than syntactic.
Why This Prevents Careless Work
Because suspicious results often come from logic problems that need diagnosis, not from typos that throw errors.


Comment-And-Highlight Diagnosis Prompt
Use This When
You want to keep the prompt attached to the code itself and ask for help inside the IDE without rebuilding context in a chat box.
Prompt
Use a comment directly above the code:

# Check whether this join can duplicate rows and explain how to verify it
headcount_payroll <- headcount |>
  left_join(payroll, by = "employee_id")

Highlight both lines, then use Ctrl + Shift + S.
What You Are Checking
Whether the highlighted code block contains a logic risk and how to validate it immediately.
Why This Prevents Careless Work
Because it keeps the prompt, the code, and the verification step together in the same workspace.
crumbs: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R

title: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R
section: B.5 The Two Shortcuts That Make This Workflow Fast
text:
B.5 The Two Shortcuts That Make This Workflow Fast
The real upgrade is not “having AI.”
The real upgrade is reducing friction at the point of work.
That is why the two shortcuts matter so much.

Ctrl + Shift + C Opens Chat
Use this when you need the chat pane open and ready inside RStudio or Positron.
This is the opening move.
You stay inside the IDE. You do not shift into browser mode. You do not rebuild the whole scenario in another tab. You keep the conversation where the work is already happening.


Ctrl + Shift + S Sends The Selected Prompt Or Highlighted Code
Use this when the question already exists in the script.
That question may be:

a block of code
a comment plus code
a drafted plain-English prompt
a small workflow fragment you want explained, fixed, or translated

This is the momentum key.
Highlight the prompt.
Or highlight the code and the comment above it.
Send it.
Stay in the editor.
That is what makes the workflow fast enough to survive real deadlines.

# Translate this business rule into tidyverse code and include verification steps
# If region is West or South, mark it as Priority Region. Otherwise mark it as Standard Region.
sales_actuals <- sales_actuals |>
  mutate(priority_group = case_when(
    region %in% c("West", "South") ~ "Priority Region",
    TRUE ~ "Standard Region"
  ))

Highlight the comment and the code. Then use Ctrl + Shift + S.
That is the workflow in its best form. The question does not leave the IDE. The assistant does not sit somewhere else waiting to be briefed. The help arrives near the object, the code, and the context.






WarningQuick Warning



If you keep opening a browser tab every time the code becomes specific, the friction returns.
You lose the Environment context.
You start copy-pasting too much.
You explain the same tables again.
You burn time describing what the IDE already knows.
That is the old trap in cleaner clothes.
crumbs: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R

title: The In-Session AI Prompt Library for R
section: B.6 Future Prompt Library Expansions
text:
B.6 Future Prompt Library Expansions
This section is intentionally structured for growth. It is not filler. It is a place to expand the in-session workflow as your use of R gets sharper and the tasks get more ambitious.

Error Diagnosis Prompt
Use This When
[Placeholder for a scenario where the code throws an error you need help interpreting quickly inside the IDE.]
Prompt
[Placeholder for an expanded in-session prompt that includes the error message, the code block, and a request for both diagnosis and safer revision.]
Why This Prompt Works
[Placeholder for tactical explanation about why including the error text, object names, and expected result improves the response.]
Verification Step
[Placeholder for the trust check you should run after applying the fix.]


Refactor This Code Prompt
Use This When
[Placeholder for a scenario where the code works but is too messy, repetitive, or hard to reuse.]
Prompt
[Placeholder for a prompt asking the assistant to rewrite the pipeline more cleanly without changing the business result.]
Why This Prompt Works
[Placeholder for a note explaining why asking for readability and reuse is better than asking for “better code” generically.]
Verification Step
[Placeholder for checking whether the refactored output matches the original totals or row counts.]


Comment-To-Code Prompt
Use This When
[Placeholder for a scenario where you know the business rule but do not want to write the code from scratch.]
Prompt
[Placeholder for a commented instruction block that can be highlighted and sent directly from the script.]
Why This Prompt Works
[Placeholder for why comments reduce friction and keep the logic attached to the workflow.]
Verification Step
[Placeholder for how to confirm the generated code did what the comment asked.]


Explain This Object In My Environment Prompt
Use This When
[Placeholder for a scenario where a data frame or object already exists, but you want help understanding its structure or likely use.]
Prompt
[Placeholder for an in-session request asking the assistant to interpret the object’s shape, key fields, and next likely steps.]
Why This Prompt Works
[Placeholder for why object-aware prompting is stronger than generic explanation.]
Verification Step
[Placeholder for checking whether the explanation matches glimpse() and your actual workflow.]


Turn This Cleaning Logic Into A Reusable Function Prompt
Use This When
[Placeholder for a scenario where you are repeating the same cleaning steps across many files.]
Prompt
[Placeholder for a request to turn an existing pipeline into a function with clear arguments and a predictable output.]
Why This Prompt Works
[Placeholder for explaining why repetition is the signal that function design may now be worth it.]
Verification Step
[Placeholder for testing the function on two different but similar files.]


Build A Plot From This Summary Prompt
Use This When
[Placeholder for a scenario where the summary table is ready, but the chart still needs to become leadership-readable.]
Prompt
[Placeholder for a prompt that names the summary table, chart purpose, audience, and desired message.]
Why This Prompt Works
[Placeholder for why message-first prompting leads to cleaner charts than “make a plot of this.”]
Verification Step
[Placeholder for checking whether the chart tells the intended story without verbal rescue.]


Help Me Interpret This Result Prompt
Use This When
[Placeholder for a scenario where the code worked and the summary exists, but the business meaning is not yet clear enough to present.]
Prompt
[Placeholder for a prompt asking the assistant to help frame the result in decision-ready language without inventing conclusions.]
Why This Prompt Works
[Placeholder for why interpretation prompts should stay anchored in the actual summary table and business question.]
Verification Step
[Placeholder for checking that the explanation stays consistent with the numbers and does not overstate certainty.]






ImportantMonday Morning Application



Choose one live task in RStudio today and run the full in-session workflow.

open chat with Ctrl + Shift + C
write the business rule in a comment or prompt block
highlight the prompt or the comment-plus-code block
send it with Ctrl + Shift + S
ask for code, explanation, and one verification step
run the verification before trusting the result

If the prompt kept you inside the IDE and got you to a trustworthy answer faster, the workflow is doing what it should.




Last Working Rule
Do not ask the assistant for brilliance.
Ask it for lower friction, cleaner translation, and help that stays close to the work.
That is how the in-session workflow becomes valuable enough to keep.
